exercises.name: Barbell Split Squat
exercises.notes: Set the barbell on a squat rack at upper‑chest height and load appropriate weight.
instructions: Set the barbell on a squat rack at upper‑chest height and load appropriate weight. Step under the bar and rest it across your upper back/traps with a secure, pronated grip; unrack the bar and take a small step back.

Position your feet in a split stance: one foot forward (working leg) and the other foot back with toes on the floor and heel elevated slightly; keep a distance so the front knee can bend to about 90 degrees at the bottom.
Brace your core and keep your torso upright and chest lifted throughout the movement; gaze forward to help maintain posture.

Slowly lower your body by bending the front knee and pushing the hips back slightly until the front thigh is around parallel to the floor or you reach a comfortable depth; the rear knee should lower toward the floor but not necessarily touch.
Ensure the front knee tracks in line with your toes (avoid collapsing inward) and keep weight primarily through the front heel and midfoot.
Drive through the front heel to extend the front knee and hip, returning to the starting split stance. Exhale on the drive.
Complete the desired number of reps on the working leg, then carefully step the back foot forward, re‑rack the bar, reset, and repeat with the opposite leg.
exercises.description: A unilateral, loaded lower‑body exercise performed in a staggered (split) stance with a barbell on the upper back that emphasizes the front leg's quadriceps while also working the glutes and hamstrings for hip control and the core for stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
instructions: Stand upright holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip, feet about hip‑width apart and toes pointing forward.
Set a slight bend in the knees (soft knees) and brace your core to stabilize the spine before initiating the movement.

Hinge at the hips by pushing your hips straight back while keeping the spine neutral and chest slightly lifted; allow the dumbbells to slide down the front of your thighs and shins, staying close to the body.
Lower until you feel a strong stretch in the hamstrings or the dumbbells reach mid‑shin (maintain neutral cervical alignment, do not round the lower back).
Reverse the motion by driving the hips forward, squeezing the glutes and hamstrings to return to standing while keeping the knees softly bent and the weights close to the legs.
Maintain controlled tempo (slow on the descent, controlled drive on the ascent), keep the shoulders down away from the ears, and avoid using the lower back to lift.
exercises.description: A standing hip‑hinge exercise performed with dumbbells that emphasizes eccentric loading of the hamstrings while the hips move back and forth; the movement also engages the glutes and core to maintain a neutral spine. Keep a slight knee bend and the weights close to the legs throughout.

exercises.name: Barbell Sumo Romanian Deadlift
exercises.notes: Brace your core, set a neutral spine (no rounding) and slightly bend the knees — this is a standing starting position.
instructions: Stand with a wide (sumo) stance, toes turned slightly outward and feet under the sleeves of the barbell so the bar is over mid-foot; grip the bar with hands roughly shoulder-width apart using an overhand or mixed grip.
Brace your core, set a neutral spine (no rounding) and slightly bend the knees — this is a standing starting position.
Begin the rep by hinging at the hips: push your hips back while keeping the barbell close to your legs, maintaining a long neutral spine and tension in the hamstrings and glutes.
Lower the bar in a controlled manner until you feel a stretch in the hamstrings or the bar reaches about mid-shin (or just below the knees), keeping the neck neutral and shoulders pulled back.
Pause briefly at the bottom while keeping the core engaged, then drive the hips forward to return to standing by contracting the glutes — do not hyperextend the lower back at the top.
Keep the knees slightly bent throughout (not a deep squat), maintain the bar close to the body on ascent and descent, and exhale as you drive the hips forward.
Use an appropriate weight that allows you to maintain a neutral spine and proper hinge mechanics for all repetitions.
exercises.description: A wide-stance (sumo) Romanian deadlift is a hip-hinge movement performed with a barbell that emphasizes the glutes while also loading the hamstrings and inner thighs; the lifter maintains a neutral spine and a slight knee bend as the bar travels close to the legs from hip height to mid-shin.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Split Squat Front Foot Elevanted
exercises.notes: Stand in a split stance with the front foot placed on a low step or platform and the rear foot on the floor with toes touching; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (standing, upright position).
instructions: Stand in a split stance with the front foot placed on a low step or platform and the rear foot on the floor with toes touching; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (standing, upright position).
Set your feet hip-width apart laterally for balance and keep your torso tall with chest up and core braced (avoid leaning forward).

Begin the descent by bending the front knee and hip, lowering your body straight down until the front thigh is about parallel to the floor or you reach a comfortable 90° knee angle; keep the rear knee soft and lowered toward the floor but not touching.
Ensure the front knee tracks in line with your toes (avoid collapsing inward) and distribute weight through the front heel and midfoot as you lower.
Pause briefly at the bottom with control, then drive through the front heel to extend the knee and hip, returning to the starting upright position.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch legs so the opposite foot becomes the front elevated foot.
Breathing: inhale as you lower, exhale as you push back up. Use a step height and dumbbell weight that allows controlled movement without losing balance.
exercises.description: A unilateral squat variation performed in a split stance with the front foot elevated on a step while holding dumbbells by your sides; it emphasizes the front leg's knee extension (quads) and challenges balance and hip control. The back foot remains on the toes while you lower and raise your body through the front hip and knee.

exercises.name: Barbell Staggered Stance Deadlift
exercises.notes: Approach the bar and grip it just outside your knees with an overhand or mixed grip; keep arms straight and shoulders over the bar.
instructions: Stand with a barbell over mid-foot and adopt a staggered stance: one foot forward flat on the floor and the other foot slightly back (toes touching or on the ball of the foot).
Approach the bar and grip it just outside your knees with an overhand or mixed grip; keep arms straight and shoulders over the bar.
Bend knees slightly and push hips back into a deep hip-hinge while maintaining a neutral spine and a braced core; keep the bar close to your shins.

Drive through the front foot (heel and midfoot) by extending the hips and knees simultaneously, squeezing the hamstrings and glutes to stand upright; keep the rear leg stable and slightly bent or on the toes for balance.
At the top, finish with hips fully extended but avoid hyperextending the lower back; shoulders should be down and back.
Lower the bar under control by hinging at the hips and allowing the knees to flex slightly, maintaining a neutral spine and keeping the bar close to the legs until it returns to the floor.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch lead legs to work the other side.
exercises.description: A single-leg biased deadlift performed from a staggered standing position that emphasizes a hip-hinge to load the hamstrings and glutes while challenging unilateral stability. The movement lifts a barbell from the floor by extending the hips and knees while keeping the torso stable.

exercises.name: Smith Seated Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Setup the Smith machine: place a flat bench in the rack so you can sit with feet on a raised block or plate with heels hanging off the edge; set the bar height so it rests across your upper thighs/knees when seated.
instructions: Setup the Smith machine: place a flat bench in the rack so you can sit with feet on a raised block or plate with heels hanging off the edge; set the bar height so it rests across your upper thighs/knees when seated.

Sit on the bench with knees bent about 90 degrees, shins vertical, feet hip-width apart and toes pointing forward on the block so only the balls of the feet contact the platform; place the Smith barpad or a pad across your upper thighs just above the knees and unhook the bar.
Brace your core, keep an upright seated posture (not slouched), and grip the bar lightly for stability; ensure your knees remain bent throughout the movement (seated position).
Lower your heels slowly by dorsiflexing the ankles until you feel a stretch in the calves (heels drop below the level of the balls of the feet), keeping control and no bouncing.
Drive through the balls of the feet and contract your calves to raise the heels as high as possible (plantarflexion), hold a brief peak contraction at the top.
Lower back down under control to the starting stretched position and repeat for the desired number of reps. Re-rack the bar safely when finished.
Notes: use a controlled tempo, avoid using knee extension or hip drive to lift the weight, and adjust foot position (toes in/out) if you want to emphasize different parts of the calf.
exercises.description: Seated calf raise performed in a Smith machine; sitting with knees bent under the bar, you raise and lower the heels to work the calf muscles through full ankle plantarflexion and dorsiflexion. This variation emphasizes the soleus portion of the calves due to the knee-flexed position.

exercises.name: Lever Standing Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Set the desired weight on the lever standing calf raise machine and step onto the foot platform so the balls of your feet are on the edge and your heels can hang freely.
instructions: Set the desired weight on the lever standing calf raise machine and step onto the foot platform so the balls of your feet are on the edge and your heels can hang freely.
Position your shoulders under the padded lever, stand tall and grip the machine handles for stability; feet should be hip-width and toes pointing forward (or slightly turned in/out to vary emphasis).
Begin with a full stretch: lower your heels down until you feel a controlled stretch in the calves (keep torso upright and spine neutral).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible, exhaling and keeping knees straight (a slight knee bend shifts emphasis to the soleus if desired).
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top and squeeze the calves, then slowly lower your heels back to the stretched starting position with control—avoid bouncing.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and proper alignment throughout.
exercises.description: A machine-based standing calf raise where the shoulders are braced under a padded lever and the heels drop and rise from a platform to load the calf muscles through a full range of motion. Emphasizes plantarflexion to strengthen and develop the gastrocnemius/soleus complex.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Calf Raise (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
instructions: Sit on the seated calf raise machine so your knees are bent at about 90 degrees and your back is upright (seated position).
Place the balls of your feet on the edge of the foot platform with heels hanging freely off the platform.
Position the padded lever across your thighs just above the knees and load the desired plates on the lever (plate-loaded).
Grasp the handles or machine grips for stability and brace your core.
Begin with your heels lowered until you feel a stretch in the calves (ankle dorsiflexion).
Drive through the balls of your feet to raise your heels as high as possible (ankle plantarflexion), lifting the lever and plates.
Pause and squeeze your calves at the top for 1–2 seconds while keeping knees bent and torso stable.
Lower your heels slowly and under control back to the starting stretched position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding bouncing or using hip/knee extension to assist the movement.
exercises.description: Seated, plate-loaded calf raise that isolates the calf musculature by plantarflexing the ankles while the knees are bent; the lever pad rests on the thighs and weight is applied via a plate-loaded lever. Performed from a seated position to emphasize the soleus and overall calf development.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Barbell Feet Flat Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
instructions: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
Lie supine on the flat bench with your eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and planted for stability, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (pinch them together and press them into the bench) to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the bar with a full grip slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over forearms and elbows under the bar.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar into the start position directly above your shoulders.
Inhale, then lower the bar in a controlled manner to mid-chest / nipple line, keeping elbows at about a 45° angle to the torso and maintaining scapular retraction.
Briefly pause with the bar lightly contacting the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar upward along a slightly arced path back to full arm extension.
Lock out the elbows at the top without overextending, keep the feet planted and core braced throughout the set.
After the final rep, carefully rack the bar onto the supports or have your spotter assist; use a spotter for heavy loads and maintain strict control on every rep.
exercises.description: Barbell feet-flat flat bench press is a supine pressing exercise that primarily works the pectorals by pressing a loaded barbell from the chest to full arm extension. Keeping the feet flat provides a stable base while the triceps and delts assist in elbow extension and shoulder stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand. Plant your feet flat on the floor and lie back so your head, upper back, and butt are supported (supine on incline bench).
Hold the dumbbells with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and press them together over your upper chest with a small bend in both elbows; this is the start position.
Keep your shoulder blades lightly retracted and braced throughout the set to protect the shoulder joint.

Lower one dumbbell in a slow, controlled arc out to the side (maintain the same elbow bend) until you feel a stretch in the upper chest; keep the opposite arm stationary and slightly elevated above the chest.
Pause briefly at the bottom for tension on the pectorals, then drive that dumbbell back up in the same arc to meet the elevated dumbbell above the chest, squeezing the pectorals at the top.
Alternate sides each repetition, maintaining control, a constant elbow angle, and avoiding bouncing or letting the lowered elbow drop behind the plane of your torso.
Breathe in as you lower the weight and exhale as you bring it back up. Use a weight that allows strict form and full control for the full range of motion.
Avoid shrugging the shoulders or overarching the lower back; stop immediately if you feel sharp shoulder pain and reduce range or load.
exercises.description: Lying supine on an incline bench, perform alternating single-arm dumbbell flyes in a wide arc to isolate the upper chest (pectorals) while one arm remains elevated as the other lowers. The movement emphasizes a controlled stretch and contraction of the upper chest with stabilization from the anterior delts and forearms.

exercises.name: Barbell Seated Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and torso vertical (back neutral).
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and torso vertical (back neutral).
Grip the barbell with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and palms facing forward; rest the bar at upper chest/clavicle level.
Brace your core and squeeze your shoulder blades down and slightly back to stabilize the spine; keep head neutral with eyes forward.
Inhale, then press the bar upward in a vertical path by extending your elbows and driving through the delts until arms are nearly locked out overhead.
As the bar passes your forehead, slightly tilt your head back to allow clearance, then return your head to neutral at the top.
Pause briefly at the top with the bar roughly over mid-foot, shoulder blades stable and ribs down to avoid excessive lower-back arching.
Exhale and slowly lower the bar under control back to the starting position at the upper chest/clavicle, keeping elbows slightly in front of the body.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a tight core and steady breathing; avoid bouncing the bar off the chest or flaring the ribs.
exercises.description: Seated barbell overhead press performed from a seated, upright position pressing a barbell from the upper chest/clavicle to full overhead lockout. Primarily targets the shoulder delts while engaging triceps and upper traps for assistance and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.

exercises.name: Barbell Wide Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench under a rack and load the barbell; position yourself lying supine on the bench with eyes roughly under the bar.
instructions: Set up a flat bench under a rack and load the barbell; position yourself lying supine on the bench with eyes roughly under the bar.
Plant feet firmly on the floor, pull your shoulder blades together and down (retract and depress) and maintain a tight core and neutral spine.
Grip the bar with a wide, pronated grip (wider than shoulder-width) so the forearms are more angled when the bar is at chest level.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your chest, keeping shoulders stable and the elbows under the bar.
Inhale, slowly lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest / nipple line while keeping the elbows tracking out at a moderate flare and the scapulae retracted.
Pause briefly with the bar lightly touching the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar back up along a slight arc until the arms are nearly extended.
Avoid locking the elbows aggressively at the top, maintain tension in the pectorals and triceps, and keep the wrists neutral.
Return the bar to the rack with control after your set; use a spotter if lifting heavy or approaching failure.
exercises.description: A barbell bench press performed with a wider-than-shoulder grip to emphasize the outer pectoral fibers. Lying supine on a flat bench, the bar is lowered to the mid-chest and pressed back to full arm extension.

exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported (supine on the incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level with palms facing forward and elbows bent (upper arms roughly 45° from the torso) while retracting the shoulder blades slightly.
Unrack the dumbbells by pressing them up and then lowering them to start position so the handles are beside your upper chest/shoulder area.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking or hyperextending the elbows); exhale during the press.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the upper chest/shoulder level while inhaling.
Keep a stable core and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; maintain scapular control and smooth, controlled reps for the desired number of repetitions.
If using heavy weights, have a spotter or perform a controlled seated dumbbell rack/unrack to avoid strain when starting or finishing the set.
exercises.description: A pressing movement performed lying supine on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest. Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion and independent arm movement compared with a barbell.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.

Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
instructions: Set an incline bench at about 30–45 degrees and position it in a rack with safety catches or a spotter available.
Lie supine on the incline bench with your head supported, feet flat on the floor, and back slightly arched while maintaining scapular retraction.
Grip the barbell with a pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your upper chest/shoulder line, then stabilize before starting the descent.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the upper chest/clavicular area, keeping elbows at roughly a 30–60° angle from the torso (avoid flaring to 90°).
Lightly touch or stop just above the chest, pause briefly without bouncing, then exhale and press the bar upward and slightly back to the start position, fully extending the elbows.
Keep core braced and feet planted throughout to maintain stability and prevent excessive lower back extension.
Carefully re-rack the bar under control or have a spotter assist when finished.
exercises.description: A barbell press performed on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest by pressing the bar upward and slightly toward the clavicle. It also engages the front delts and triceps for pressing and locking out the weight.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.

Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell by each knee.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell by each knee.
Lie back so your head, upper back and glutes are supported (supine on incline) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Pick up the dumbbells and bring them to your sides (low position) with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows; retract the shoulder blades slightly to protect the shoulders.
Initiate the movement by squeezing the pectorals and lifting the dumbbells in a wide arc toward each other above the upper chest, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout.
Pause briefly at the top with the dumbbells close but not forcefully smashed together; exhale during the concentric (lifting) phase.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back down along the same arc to the low starting position under control, inhaling during the descent and maintaining tension in the chest.
Keep the torso stable (avoid excessive lumbar arching or using leg drive) and limit range if you feel shoulder pain; perform all reps with controlled tempo.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions while focusing on chest contraction rather than moving the weight with momentum.
exercises.description: Lying on an incline bench, perform a dumbbell fly from a low starting position to emphasize the upper chest (pectorals) by arcing the arms upward and together. Keep a small bend in the elbows and control the descent to maintain tension on the chest.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back so your upper back and head are supported (supine on the incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability.
Bring the dumbbells up to shoulder level with palms facing forward and elbows bent (upper arms roughly 45° from the torso) while retracting the shoulder blades slightly.
Unrack the dumbbells by pressing them up and then lowering them to start position so the handles are beside your upper chest/shoulder area.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are nearly straight (avoid locking or hyperextending the elbows); exhale during the press.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the upper chest/shoulder level while inhaling.
Keep a stable core and avoid excessive arching of the lower back; maintain scapular control and smooth, controlled reps for the desired number of repetitions.
If using heavy weights, have a spotter or perform a controlled seated dumbbell rack/unrack to avoid strain when starting or finishing the set.
exercises.description: A pressing movement performed lying supine on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the chest. Dumbbells allow a slightly greater range of motion and independent arm movement compared with a barbell.

exercises.name: Lever Crossovers
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest level when you sit upright.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handles are at mid-chest level when you sit upright.
Sit with your back pressed firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor and knees at about 90°.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-down grip and start with your arms opened out to the sides at shoulder height, elbows slightly bent.
Brace your core and keep your torso stable; avoid leaning forward or arching the lower back.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a slow, controlled arc in front of your chest, allowing the hands to cross slightly if the machine path allows.
Pause briefly at full contraction and focus on squeezing the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position, maintaining the slight bend in the elbows and control throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping tension on the chest and avoiding momentum.
exercises.description: Seated lever crossovers are a machine chest fly variation that brings the arms together in front of the body to isolate and load the pectorals while minimizing torso movement. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and a controlled arc to squeeze the chest at peak contraction.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise (support head)
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and let the arms hang straight down beneath your shoulders; maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Begin the movement by initiating at the shoulders: raise both dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc with a slight bend in the elbows, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Continue lifting until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (or in line with your shoulders), keeping the wrists neutral and avoiding excessive elbow lock.
Pause and squeeze the rear delts/upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the head and torso supported on the bench.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position under control, resisting any tendency to let momentum drop the weights.
Breathe out as you lift and inhale as you lower. Maintain a stable torso—do not rotate, jerk, or shrug the shoulders during the movement.
Use lighter weight if you cannot perform the motion with controlled form or if you feel neck or lumbar strain despite the head support.
exercises.description: A bent-over rear lateral raise performed with the head supported on an incline bench to isolate the posterior delts; lift dumbbells out to the sides while keeping the torso stable to target the rear shoulder and upper back. This variation reduces momentum and neck strain by using the bench for support.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.

It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Cable Incline Y Raise
exercises.notes: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
instructions: Set a bench to an incline (about 30–45°) and position it so your back is fully supported against the pad.
Attach wrist straps to low pulley handles (both sides) and set a light–moderate weight so you can control the entire range.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest against the bench, and grasp the wrist straps so your arms hang down in front of you with a slight bend in the elbows; palms should face each other.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the scapulae and maintain a neutral spine throughout the set.
Exhale and raise your arms up and out in a diagonal plane (about 30–45° forward of the frontal plane) to form a Y with your body; lead with the thumbs and keep elbows soft.
Lift until your arms are roughly in line with your head/ears (or you feel a solid contraction in the delts/traps), squeezing the upper back at the top.
Inhale and lower the arms slowly and under control back to the start position, resisting the cable’s pull.

Perform the desired reps while avoiding momentum, excessive lumbar extension, or letting the shoulders excessively shrug; keep the back supported by the bench and use wrist straps to minimize grip fatigue.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench with back support, you use low-set cables and wrist straps to lift the arms into a Y shape overhead, emphasizing the shoulder delts and the upper back/trap region. The movement is performed with a controlled lift and slow lowering to isolate the posterior/lateral delts and scapular stabilizers.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Clean and Press
exercises.notes: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Step 1: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart, toes slightly turned out, barbell over mid-foot; grip the bar just outside the knees with a full overhand or mixed grip and keep the chest up and core braced (standing position).
Step 2: Initiate the lift with a hip hinge by bending at the hips and knees keeping the spine neutral and shoulders slightly in front of the bar; maintain a tight core and engaged lats.

Step 3: Drive through the heels and extend the hips, knees and ankles explosively (triple extension) to accelerate the bar upward while shrugging the shoulders and pulling the elbows high and out (clean pull).

Step 4: As the bar reaches peak upward momentum, quickly drop under the bar into a partial front squat and rotate the elbows forward to receive the bar on the front of the shoulders in a front-rack position (clean, front-rack).

Step 5: Stand tall from the front-rack by extending the hips and knees to full standing, reset your core and grip, then press the bar overhead by extending the elbows and driving the head slightly forward until arms are fully locked out (press).
Step 6: Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position, then carefully return the bar to the floor with a hip hinge and bend the knees to reset for the next repetition.

Step 7: Maintain braced abs and a neutral spine throughout, breathe consistently (exhale during the press), and use controlled tempo—perform the clean explosively but receive and press with stability and control.
exercises.description: A compound Olympic-style lift combining a barbell clean (lifting the bar to a front-rack position) followed immediately by a press overhead, training the shoulders along with legs, hips and upper back. It develops explosive power, pressing strength, and full-body coordination.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Front Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back firmly supported and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back firmly supported and feet flat on the floor.
Pick up a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or overhand grip and let your arms hang straight down at your sides, wrists neutral and a slight bend in the elbows.

Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and exhale as you raise both dumbbells together in front of you in a controlled arc to about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Keep the elbows slightly bent and wrists firm; avoid swinging or using momentum from the torso or legs.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, choosing a weight that allows strict form; stop if you feel shoulder joint pain.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, both dumbbells are raised straight forward to shoulder height to emphasize the anterior portion of the delts. The incline and controlled motion reduce momentum and isolate the front shoulder muscles.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single handles. Stand centered between the two towers and grasp each handle with the opposite hand so the cables cross in front of you (or hold same-side handles if preferred).
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart, knees soft, and core braced (neutral spine). Keep a slight bend in the elbows and wrists neutral.
Start with the handles low by your thighs; palms can face inward or slightly forward. Maintain a small forward lean from the hips if comfortable to allow a natural arc.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, exhale and raise both arms out to the sides in a smooth arc until your hands reach approximately shoulder height. Lead the movement with the elbow, not the wrist.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts; avoid shrugging the shoulders upward toward your ears.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly and under control back to the starting position, keeping tension on the cables throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Use a lighter weight if you cannot maintain strict form or if you begin using torso momentum.
exercises.description: A standing cable lateral raise performed from low pulleys to isolate the lateral head of the delts; cables provide constant tension while you lift the arms out to the sides to shoulder height. The movement is done with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to minimize momentum.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
instructions: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
Lie supine on the decline bench so your head is lower than your hips and your shoulders and back are flat against the bench; keep feet locked under the pads for stability.
Grasp the bar with a double overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width (pronated grip); wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar out over your lower chest; maintain a controlled, tight torso and scapular retraction.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the lower chest/upper abdomen (sternal/xyphoid area) with elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest, then exhale and press the bar upward along the same path until arms are nearly extended.
Avoid flaring the elbows excessively and keep the feet anchored under the pads; use a spotter for heavy sets and re-rack the bar carefully after the set.
exercises.description: A decline barbell bench press performed lying supine on a decline bench with the head lower than the hips to emphasize the lower portion of the pectorals. The lifter lowers the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen and presses it back up to full extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Lateral Raise (support head)
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
instructions: Set an incline bench so the pad is angled and stand facing the bench; hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees and rest your forehead (or chest/upper stomach) lightly against the bench pad to support the head and torso.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and let the arms hang straight down beneath your shoulders; maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Begin the movement by initiating at the shoulders: raise both dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc with a slight bend in the elbows, leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Continue lifting until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (or in line with your shoulders), keeping the wrists neutral and avoiding excessive elbow lock.
Pause and squeeze the rear delts/upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the head and torso supported on the bench.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position under control, resisting any tendency to let momentum drop the weights.
Breathe out as you lift and inhale as you lower. Maintain a stable torso—do not rotate, jerk, or shrug the shoulders during the movement.
Use lighter weight if you cannot perform the motion with controlled form or if you feel neck or lumbar strain despite the head support.
exercises.description: A bent-over rear lateral raise performed with the head supported on an incline bench to isolate the posterior delts; lift dumbbells out to the sides while keeping the torso stable to target the rear shoulder and upper back. This variation reduces momentum and neck strain by using the bench for support.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30-45° incline and position it inside a power rack or with a spotter and place the barbell on the rack at a height you can reach while seated.
Sit with your back and head firmly against the bench, feet flat on the floor, and brace your core (tighten abs and glutes) to maintain a stable, slight arch in the lower back.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar directly above your upper chest/shoulder line; have a spotter if needed.
Inhale, bend the elbows, and lower the bar under control to the upper chest/clavicle area (touch point just below the top of the sternum) keeping elbows at roughly 30–60° from the torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom without bouncing, ensuring the forearms remain vertical and the core stays braced.
Exhale and press the bar back up in a slightly backward arc until the arms are fully extended but not locked aggressively at the elbows.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, then carefully re-rack the bar on the uprights with control or have the spotter assist.
exercises.description: The barbell incline bench press is a compound pressing movement performed on an inclined bench that emphasizes the upper portion of the pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps. It is done from a supine position on an incline bench, pressing a barbell vertically from chest to full arm extension.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Hammer Press
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and select two dumbbells of appropriate weight.
instructions: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and select two dumbbells of appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench and plant your feet flat on the floor; lie back so your head, upper back and hips are supported (supine on an incline bench).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip; bring the dumbbells to shoulder level with elbows bent (~90°) and wrists stacked over elbows.
Brace your core, retract your shoulder blades slightly, and keep the elbows under the wrists (avoid excessive flaring).
Exhale and press both dumbbells up and slightly together until your arms are almost extended — do not lock the elbows at the top.
Pause briefly at the top, then inhale and lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner back to shoulder level, keeping the same neutral grip.
Repeat for the desired number of reps while maintaining steady tempo, stable bench contact, and neutral wrist alignment.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, dumbbells are pressed upward with a neutral (hammer) grip to emphasize the upper chest. The movement uses a controlled press and descent to work the pectorals with assistance from the delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward (or slightly rotated) and elbows bent roughly 90 degrees, wrists stacked over elbows.
Inhale, then exhale as you press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until your arms are nearly extended overhead; avoid fully locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the shoulders down and chest lifted (do not shrug excessively).
Inhale as you slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Keep the back pressed against the bench and avoid excessive lower-back arching or leaning; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press performed from a back-supported bench to drive the weights overhead, primarily working the shoulder (deltoid) muscles with triceps and upper traps assisting. The movement emphasizes vertical pressing strength and shoulder stability.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
