exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

exercises.name: Landmine Shoulders Press with Viking Press Attachment
exercises.notes: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grip the Viking press handles so the attachment sits at shoulder height with elbows roughly tucked under the wrists.
instructions: Stand facing the landmine with feet shoulder-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; grip the Viking press handles so the attachment sits at shoulder height with elbows roughly tucked under the wrists.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neutral spine, and position the bar angled away from you so it can travel upward without hitting your head.
Inhale, then press the handles upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your arms are fully extended overhead or slightly behind the head, avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders engaged and elbows unlocked but not hyperextended.
Exhale as you slowly lower the handles back down to the starting shoulder-height position under control, maintaining tension in the delts and core.
Repeat for the desired reps; keep movement smooth and controlled, avoid leaning the torso excessively back or using lower-body drive to move the load.
Adjust stance or hand position to shift emphasis (wider grip = more lateral delt emphasis; closer grip = more anterior delt/pectorals) and lower the weight if you cannot maintain strict form.
exercises.description: A standing landmine press using a Viking press attachment that emphasizes overhead pressing from the shoulders; the movement drives the handles upward from shoulder height to full arm extension, targeting the delts while the triceps and upper chest assist.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
instructions: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
Lie supine on the decline bench so your head is lower than your hips and your shoulders and back are flat against the bench; keep feet locked under the pads for stability.
Grasp the bar with a double overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width (pronated grip); wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar out over your lower chest; maintain a controlled, tight torso and scapular retraction.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the lower chest/upper abdomen (sternal/xyphoid area) with elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest, then exhale and press the bar upward along the same path until arms are nearly extended.
Avoid flaring the elbows excessively and keep the feet anchored under the pads; use a spotter for heavy sets and re-rack the bar carefully after the set.
exercises.description: A decline barbell bench press performed lying supine on a decline bench with the head lower than the hips to emphasize the lower portion of the pectorals. The lifter lowers the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen and presses it back up to full extension.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Shoulders Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and knees slightly soft.
Place the barbell in a front-rack position resting on the upper chest/clavicle area with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width and full grip around the bar.
Retract the shoulder blades slightly, keep the chest up and core braced (neutral spine); avoid excessive lumbar arching.
Take a full breath and begin the press by driving the bar upward in a straight line, slightly moving the head back as the bar passes and then returning the head forward under the bar.
Fully extend the elbows at the top with the bar directly over or slightly behind the head, shoulders stacked over hips and feet for balance.
Lower the bar under control back to the front-rack position to complete one rep while inhaling, keeping the elbows tracking slightly forward.
Repeat for desired repetitions, maintaining tight core engagement and controlled tempo; stop or reduce weight if you must excessively lean back to finish reps.
exercises.description: A standing barbell overhead press performed from the front-rack position that primarily develops the delts while requiring the triceps and upper back to assist and stabilize. The lifter presses the barbell from shoulder level to full arm extension overhead while maintaining a braced core.

exercises.name: Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
instructions: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the torso before initiating the pull.
Drive your elbows down and back by contracting your biceps and lats, pulling your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or the bar touches your upper chest, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the working muscles (biceps and upper back).
Lower yourself slowly and with control back to the full hang, extending the arms fully while maintaining shoulder blade stability.
Breathe out during the ascent and inhale during the controlled descent.
If you cannot complete a full chin-up, use a band-assisted variation, a box/step for negative-only reps, or an assisted chin-up machine to reduce load while maintaining the same body position.
exercises.description: A vertical pulling exercise performed from a hanging position with a supinated (underhand) grip that primarily loads the biceps while also working the lats and upper back. Pull your chin over the bar and lower under control to build upper-body pulling strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Fly
exercises.notes: Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor (or a 30–45° decline), keep a neutral spine and neck, and brace your abs.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a bench with feet flat about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is nearly parallel to the floor (or a 30–45° decline), keep a neutral spine and neck, and brace your abs.
Allow the dumbbells to hang beneath your shoulders with a slight bend in the elbows; maintain that elbow angle throughout the set.

Exhale and lift the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc by externally rotating the shoulders and squeezing the shoulder blades together; raise until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Pause briefly at the top focusing on contracting the rear delts and upper back, then inhale and lower the weights slowly back to the starting position with control.
Avoid swinging the torso or using momentum, do not shrug the shoulders up toward the ears, and keep the movement driven by the shoulders/scapulae rather than the lower back.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell rear fly is a bent-over horizontal shoulder abduction movement that targets the rear delts and upper back by pulling the arms out to the sides while keeping the torso stable. It strengthens posterior shoulder muscles and scapular retractors with a controlled, rowing-like motion.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.

exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Pull-up
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate assistance (counterweight) on the assisted pull-up/dip machine and lower the knee pad to a comfortable height.
instructions: Select an appropriate assistance (counterweight) on the assisted pull-up/dip machine and lower the knee pad to a comfortable height.
Stand facing the bar and grasp it with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Mount the machine and kneel on the pad with knees supported and feet either crossed or tucked behind; allow the arms to fully extend so your body hangs straight.
Brace your core, keep your head neutral, and pull your shoulder blades down and back to initiate the movement.
Exhale and pull yourself upward by driving your elbows down and back, bringing your chin or upper chest toward the bar while squeezing the lats and upper back.
Pause briefly at the top with the scapulae retracted and avoid excessive shrugging of the delts.
Inhale and slowly lower yourself under control until your arms are fully extended and shoulders are not shrugged.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; reduce assistance (use a lighter counterweight) as you get stronger to increase difficulty.
exercises.description: Assisted pull-up on a counterweight machine where the user kneels on the support pad and pulls their body up to the bar; this exercise primarily targets the lats while also working the pulling muscles of the arms and upper back.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
instructions: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the torso before initiating the pull.
Drive your elbows down and back by contracting your biceps and lats, pulling your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or the bar touches your upper chest, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the working muscles (biceps and upper back).
Lower yourself slowly and with control back to the full hang, extending the arms fully while maintaining shoulder blade stability.
Breathe out during the ascent and inhale during the controlled descent.
If you cannot complete a full chin-up, use a band-assisted variation, a box/step for negative-only reps, or an assisted chin-up machine to reduce load while maintaining the same body position.
exercises.description: A vertical pulling exercise performed from a hanging position with a supinated (underhand) grip that primarily loads the biceps while also working the lats and upper back. Pull your chin over the bar and lower under control to build upper-body pulling strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Lateral Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a neutral grip (palms facing your body) and a slight bend in the elbows.
Keep your torso still and shoulders down (do not shrug) throughout the movement.
Exhale and raise both arms out to the sides by leading with the elbows until your arms are approximately parallel to the floor (shoulder height).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a slight elbow bend and neutral wrist alignment.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and controlled back to the starting position at your sides.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding swinging or leaning the torso to lift heavier weights.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell lateral raises isolate the lateral delts by lifting the arms out to the sides from a seated, back-supported position. Performed with a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to emphasize the shoulder without using momentum.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Sumo Deadlift High Pull
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower into a deep squat (sumo position) until your hips are below or near knee level; keep chest up and maintain a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with a wide (sumo) stance: feet wider than shoulder-width, toes turned slightly outward and bar over the midfoot.
Hinge at the hips and bend the knees to lower into a deep squat (sumo position) until your hips are below or near knee level; keep chest up and maintain a neutral spine.
Grip the bar with both hands inside your knees (narrow grip) and engage your core; shoulders should be slightly in front of the bar.
Take a full breath and brace, then initiate the movement by driving through the heels and extending the hips and knees explosively.

Keep the bar close to your body as you extend; as the bar passes the knees, perform an explosive shrug and pull with the elbows driving upward and outward to bring the bar toward chest/chin height (high pull).
Aim to lead with the elbows so the forearms and delts receive the pull; fully extend hips at the top and stand tall without hyperextending the lower back.
Lower the bar under control by reversing the motion: allow the hips to hinge, knees to bend, and guide the bar back to the floor while maintaining a neutral spine.
Reset stance and breathing between reps; inhale and brace before each lift and exhale at/after the top. Maintain knee tracking over toes and avoid rounding the back.
exercises.description: A compound, explosive lift performed from a wide (sumo) stance that combines a deadlift hip/leg drive with an upright high pull to bring the bar toward the chest. It trains lower-body extension and upper-body pulling (shoulder shrug and elbow lift) in a single fluid movement.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Raise
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and sit with your back flat against the pad, feet planted on the floor.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms fully extended down at your sides and palms facing inward; keep a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and neck neutral, and avoid arching your lower back.
Exhale and lift both dumbbells upward and slightly forward in a controlled arc until your hands reach about shoulder height (arms roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top, maintaining tension in the delts and avoiding momentum or swinging.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting hanging position under control.
Perform the desired repetitions, keeping movement smooth and avoiding use of torso momentum; reduce weight if you need to swing to complete reps.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline bench, this variation of the dumbbell raise emphasizes the front portion of the shoulder (anterior delts) by lifting the weights from a hanging position up to about shoulder height. The incline angle shifts some load onto the chest (pectorals) while isolating the delts and minimizing torso swing.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Landmine Squat and Press
exercises.notes: Set up the landmine by securing one end of the barbell in the pivot and load the free end with appropriate weight; stand facing the bar with feet about shoulder‑width apart and toes slightly turned out.
instructions: Set up the landmine by securing one end of the barbell in the pivot and load the free end with appropriate weight; stand facing the bar with feet about shoulder‑width apart and toes slightly turned out.
Grip the end of the bar with both hands and hold it close to your chest in a front‑rack/goblet position (elbows tucked under the bar, forearms vertical) while standing tall and bracing your abs.
Begin the movement by pushing your hips back slightly and bending at the knees to lower into a controlled squat until your thighs are roughly parallel to the floor or a comfortable depth is reached.

Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand up explosively; as you reach near full hip and knee extension, press the bar upward and slightly forward using your delts and triceps until your arms are extended.
Pause briefly at the top with a stable torso, then slowly lower the bar back to the chest/front‑rack position under control and immediately move into the next squat.
Maintain a neutral spine, keep the chest up, breathe in before the descent and exhale during the press, and avoid letting the knees cave inwards throughout the movement.
exercises.description: A front‑loaded compound movement performed with a landmine bar that combines a squat with an overhead/forward press, loading the quads during the descent and the shoulders/triceps during the press. It trains lower‑body strength and upper‑body pressing in a single fluid motion.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Single Arm Row
exercises.notes: Keep your spine neutral, neck in line with the spine, and the non‑working hand resting on your thigh or a bench for support if needed.
instructions: Stand with feet hip‑width apart holding a dumbbell in one hand; bend the knees slightly and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent‑over position).
Keep your spine neutral, neck in line with the spine, and the non‑working hand resting on your thigh or a bench for support if needed.
Let the working arm hang straight down from the shoulder with the palm facing in (neutral grip).
Brace your core and pull the dumbbell straight up toward your hip/waist by driving the elbow back and squeezing the shoulder blade toward the spine; keep the elbow close to the body.
Pause briefly at the top with the scapula retracted, then lower the weight under control until the arm is fully extended.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Maintain the hip hinge and avoid rotating the torso or using momentum to lift the weight.
exercises.description: A single‑arm bent‑over row is a standing hip‑hinge rowing movement that pulls a dumbbell toward the hip to develop the lats and upper back while also working the biceps and forearms. It is performed from a bent‑over position with a neutral spine and controlled eccentric lowering.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Hammer Curl to Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, core braced and a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip at your sides.
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees soft, core braced and a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing each other) grip at your sides.
Keep your elbows close to your torso and curl the dumbbells upward in a controlled hammer curl until the dumbbells reach shoulder level.
At the top of the curl, pause briefly; if comfortable, rotate the wrists so the palms face forward or maintain a neutral grip depending on mobility.
Press the dumbbells overhead by extending the elbows and driving through the shoulders until arms are fully extended but not locked, exhaling as you press.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder level, then reverse the curl motion to return the weights to your sides, inhaling on the descent.
Maintain a neutral spine and avoid overarching the lower back—keep the core engaged and perform each rep in a smooth, controlled manner.
exercises.description: A standing combination move that pairs a hammer curl with an overhead dumbbell press to work the shoulder muscles while also recruiting the biceps and forearms. Perform a neutral-grip curl to shoulder height, then continue the motion into an overhead press and lower under control.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Y Raise
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45 degrees above parallel (bent-over position) with a slight bend in the knees; keep a neutral spine and neck (look at a point on the floor a few feet ahead).
instructions: Stand with feet hip-width apart holding a light dumbbell in each hand, palms facing each other (neutral grip).

Hinge at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45 degrees above parallel (bent-over position) with a slight bend in the knees; keep a neutral spine and neck (look at a point on the floor a few feet ahead).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with elbows soft (slight bend) and shoulders relaxed away from the ears.

Initiate the movement by externally rotating the shoulders and lifting the dumbbells overhead and outward into a Y shape (hands move diagonally away from the body at about a 30–45° angle from the torso) until arms are roughly in line with the torso or slightly above shoulder level.
Squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top without shrugging; keep the core braced to prevent low-back extension.
Lower the dumbbells slowly and under control back to the starting hanging position, maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine.
Repeat for the desired reps; choose light weights to maintain strict form and avoid momentum or excessive back arching.
exercises.description: A bent-over Y raise is a standing hip-hinged exercise that lifts light dumbbells in a Y shape to train the rear and lateral delts while improving upper-back stability and scapular control. It emphasizes controlled scapular retraction and external rotation through the shoulder joint.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

instructions: Stand beneath the dip cage and grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), hands about shoulder-width apart; step or jump up until arms are fully extended and you are in a full hang. Cross or bend your legs behind you if needed to avoid touching the floor.
Brace your core and begin by depressing and retracting the shoulder blades (scapular pull) to engage the lats before bending the elbows.

Pull your body upward by driving the elbows down and back toward your ribs, leading with the chest until your chin clears the bar or your chest reaches the handles; keep the torso relatively upright and avoid excessive swinging or kipping.
Hold the top position briefly with shoulders packed (down and back), then slowly lower yourself under control back to a full dead hang with arms fully extended.
Breathe out as you pull up and inhale as you lower; maintain a steady tempo and repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on lat contraction rather than momentum.
exercises.description: A neutral (hammer) grip pull-up performed on a dip/pull-up station that emphasizes the lats while reducing strain on the shoulders by using palms-facing-each-other handles. Performed from a dead hang and pulling the chest toward the bar with controlled descent.

exercises.name: Barbell Feet Flat Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
instructions: Set the bench inside a rack so the bar sits at arm height when you lie down; load the bar and secure collars or have a spotter.
Lie supine on the flat bench with your eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and planted for stability, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades (pinch them together and press them into the bench) to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the bar with a full grip slightly wider than shoulder width, wrists stacked over forearms and elbows under the bar.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar into the start position directly above your shoulders.
Inhale, then lower the bar in a controlled manner to mid-chest / nipple line, keeping elbows at about a 45° angle to the torso and maintaining scapular retraction.
Briefly pause with the bar lightly contacting the chest (do not bounce), then exhale and press the bar upward along a slightly arced path back to full arm extension.
Lock out the elbows at the top without overextending, keep the feet planted and core braced throughout the set.
After the final rep, carefully rack the bar onto the supports or have your spotter assist; use a spotter for heavy loads and maintain strict control on every rep.
exercises.description: Barbell feet-flat flat bench press is a supine pressing exercise that primarily works the pectorals by pressing a loaded barbell from the chest to full arm extension. Keeping the feet flat provides a stable base while the triceps and delts assist in elbow extension and shoulder stabilization.

workout.description: Full Body workout focusing on strength and endurance training.

exercises.name: Barbell Incline Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a loaded barbell within reach on the floor or on low supports.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a loaded barbell within reach on the floor or on low supports.
Lie face down (prone) on the bench so your chest is supported, feet braced on the floor or bench for stability, and your torso is fixed against the pad.
Reach down and grasp the barbell with an overhand (pronated) grip about shoulder-width apart. Allow your arms to hang fully extended.
Before pulling, draw your shoulder blades down and together (scapular retraction) to start the movement from the back rather than the arms.
Pull the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows back and slightly out.
At the top, squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold briefly (about 1 second) while keeping the torso pressed to the bench and neck neutral.
Lower the barbell slowly back to full arm extension under control, maintaining scapular tension and avoiding any bouncing or trunk rotation.
Breathe out during the pull and inhale while lowering. Keep movement strict—do not jerk the weight or lift your chest off the bench.
exercises.description: Prone row performed on an incline bench where the torso is supported and a barbell is pulled toward the lower chest/upper abdomen. It emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while also engaging the upper back, rear delts and arm muscles.

exercises.name: Reverse grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Set up: grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
instructions: Set up: grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower.
Start position: hang fully extended with feet off the ground (legs can be crossed at the ankles or slightly bent) and scapula slightly depressed and retracted to remove slack.
Initiate the pull by driving the elbows down and back, engaging the lats and upper back rather than just curling with the arms.
Continue pulling until your chin passes the bar or your upper chest reaches the bar, keeping the torso as upright and controlled as possible.
Pause briefly at the top while squeezing the lats and upper back, avoiding excessive neck or shoulder shrugging.
Lower slowly and under control back to a full dead hang (arms fully extended) while maintaining scapular control.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Breathe out on the pull and inhale as you lower. Avoid kipping or excessive swinging to keep tension on the target muscles.
exercises.description: A supinated (underhand) grip pull-up that emphasizes the latissimus and recruits the biceps more than a pronated pull-up. From a dead hang you pull your torso up until your chin or chest reaches the bar, then lower with control.

exercises.name: Barbell Close Grip Larsen Press
exercises.notes: Set a barbell in a rack at a height you can safely unrack while lying on a flat bench; load appropriate weight and use a spotter or safety pins.
instructions: Set a barbell in a rack at a height you can safely unrack while lying on a flat bench; load appropriate weight and use a spotter or safety pins.
Lie supine on the bench with your eyes roughly under the bar, feet planted flat on the floor for stability, and shoulder blades squeezed together.
Grip the bar with a close/narrow grip (hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower) with thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Unrack the bar and hold it with arms extended over the sternum, wrists stacked over elbows and bar positioned vertically above your chest.
Lower the bar under control to the upper-to-mid chest (sternal/clavicular area) while keeping elbows tucked closer to your body to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to lightly rest on the chest (brief pause) — this is the Larsen touch-and-rest — then drive the bar upward by pressing through the chest and triceps until arms are nearly extended.
Keep a tight torso and steady breathing: inhale on the descent, exhale as you press up. Repeat for desired reps.
Re‑rack the bar under control at the end of the set, making sure the bar is fully seated on the rack supports.
exercises.description: A close‑grip variation of the bench press where the bar is briefly rested on the chest (Larsen style) between reps; emphasizes the inner chest while also heavily recruiting the triceps and front delts. Performed lying supine on a flat bench using a narrow hand position.

exercises.name: Hammer Grip Pull-up on Dip Cage
exercises.notes: Stand beneath the dip cage and grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), hands about shoulder-width apart; step or jump up until arms are fully extended and you are in a full hang.
instructions: Stand beneath the dip cage and grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), hands about shoulder-width apart; step or jump up until arms are fully extended and you are in a full hang. Cross or bend your legs behind you if needed to avoid touching the floor.
Brace your core and begin by depressing and retracting the shoulder blades (scapular pull) to engage the lats before bending the elbows.

Pull your body upward by driving the elbows down and back toward your ribs, leading with the chest until your chin clears the bar or your chest reaches the handles; keep the torso relatively upright and avoid excessive swinging or kipping.
Hold the top position briefly with shoulders packed (down and back), then slowly lower yourself under control back to a full dead hang with arms fully extended.
Breathe out as you pull up and inhale as you lower; maintain a steady tempo and repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on lat contraction rather than momentum.
exercises.description: A neutral (hammer) grip pull-up performed on a dip/pull-up station that emphasizes the lats while reducing strain on the shoulders by using palms-facing-each-other handles. Performed from a dead hang and pulling the chest toward the bar with controlled descent.

exercises.name: Shoulder Grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grip it with an overhand (palms facing away) shoulder-width grip.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grip it with an overhand (palms facing away) shoulder-width grip.
Begin in a dead hang with arms fully extended, legs either straight or lightly crossed at the ankles, core braced and shoulders slightly depressed (scapulae down and back).
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades down and together, then drive your elbows down and back while squeezing the lats to lift your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar (or chest approaches the bar), keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging or kipping.
Lower yourself back to a full dead hang in a slow, controlled manner until arms are fully extended and scapulae are back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps — exhale during the upward (concentric) phase and inhale as you lower (eccentric) — maintain tension in the core and avoid shrugging the shoulders at the top.
exercises.description: A shoulder-width overhand pull-up where you hang from the bar and pull your body up using your back and arms. Focuses on vertical pulling to develop the lats while the biceps and upper back assist.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Cable Pushdown (straight arm)
exercises.notes: Attach a straight bar, rope, or single handle to a high pulley.
instructions: Attach a straight bar, rope, or single handle to a high pulley. Select a moderate weight that allows controlled movement while keeping arms mostly straight.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart (or a slight stagger for balance). Grasp the handle with both hands (pronated or neutral grip) and step back slightly so the cable is taut.

Hinge slightly forward at the hips so your torso leans about 10–20 degrees; keep knees soft and maintain a neutral spine. Arms should be extended in front of you with a small, fixed bend in the elbows (not fully locked).

Initiate the rep by driving the shoulders into extension: press the hands downward and back in a controlled arc until the hands reach thigh level and you feel maximal contraction in the lats. Keep the elbows stationary and avoid bending them to pull.
Pause briefly at the bottom while squeezing the lats and maintaining straight-arm tension.
Slowly return the handle to the starting position under control, keeping constant tension on the cable and avoiding letting the shoulders fully relax at the top.
Breathe out during the downward (working) phase and inhale while returning to the start. Perform desired number of reps with steady tempo.
exercises.description: Standing straight-arm cable pushdowns target the lats by driving the arms down from a high cable with minimal elbow flexion. The movement emphasizes shoulder extension and scapular retraction while keeping the torso stable.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Cable Rope Seated Row
exercises.notes: Grasp the rope attachment with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and sit upright with a neutral spine and chest up; start with arms fully extended.
instructions: Sit on a seated row bench or platform with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Grasp the rope attachment with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and sit upright with a neutral spine and chest up; start with arms fully extended.
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction) and drive your elbows straight back toward your torso.
Pull the rope toward your upper abdomen or lower chest, keeping elbows close to the body and leading with the elbows rather than the hands.
Pause briefly at peak contraction while squeezing the lats and upper back, maintaining an upright torso (avoid excessive trunk lean or momentum).
Slowly extend the arms back to the starting position with control, returning the shoulders to a slightly protracted position while keeping the spine neutral.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, ensuring wrists remain neutral and movements are smooth and controlled.
exercises.description: Seated cable rope row performed from a bench using a rope attachment to pull the handle toward the torso, emphasizing scapular retraction and elbow drive to work the lats and upper back. Keeps a neutral spine and controlled tempo to isolate the pulling muscles.

exercises.name: Cable Single Arm High Row
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a high cable pulley.
instructions: Set a single handle on a high cable pulley. Adjust a bench or chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and you can sit upright without leaning forward or rotating.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest pressed into the support, and grab the handle with a neutral or overhand grip using one arm; start with the arm extended and shoulder slightly protracted.
Brace your core, keep your torso stationary against the chest support, and retract your shoulder blade slightly before initiating the pull.
Pull the handle down and back in a smooth arc by driving your elbow up and back toward your ribcage, focusing on squeezing the upper back and rear delt at the end range.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction while maintaining a neutral wrist and no torso rotation or shrugging, then slowly extend the arm back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Maintain steady breathing (exhale during the pull, inhale during the return) and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: A single-arm high cable row performed with chest support to isolate the upper back while minimizing torso movement. The movement pulls a high-set handle down and back with the elbow driving toward the ribcage to emphasize the upper back and rear delts.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Shoulder Grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grip it with an overhand (palms facing away) shoulder-width grip.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grip it with an overhand (palms facing away) shoulder-width grip.
Begin in a dead hang with arms fully extended, legs either straight or lightly crossed at the ankles, core braced and shoulders slightly depressed (scapulae down and back).
Initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades down and together, then drive your elbows down and back while squeezing the lats to lift your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar (or chest approaches the bar), keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging or kipping.
Lower yourself back to a full dead hang in a slow, controlled manner until arms are fully extended and scapulae are back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps — exhale during the upward (concentric) phase and inhale as you lower (eccentric) — maintain tension in the core and avoid shrugging the shoulders at the top.
exercises.description: A shoulder-width overhand pull-up where you hang from the bar and pull your body up using your back and arms. Focuses on vertical pulling to develop the lats while the biceps and upper back assist.

exercises.name: Lever Incline Hammer Chest Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine seat so your back is fully supported on the incline pad and the handles start roughly at upper-chest / clavicle level when grasped.
instructions: Adjust the machine seat so your back is fully supported on the incline pad and the handles start roughly at upper-chest / clavicle level when grasped.
Select an appropriate weight and sit with your feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and your lower back gently pressed into the bench (avoid excessive arching).
Reach up and grasp the handles with a neutral (palms facing each other) 'hammer' grip; pull your shoulder blades slightly down and back to stabilize the chest.
Begin with elbows bent and positioned at about a 30–45° angle from the torso (not flared fully out), wrists neutral and aligned with forearms.
Exhale and press the handles upward and slightly together in a controlled motion until your arms are nearly extended — avoid locking the elbows at the top.
Pause briefly at the top and consciously squeeze the upper pectorals while maintaining scapular stability.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly back to the start position, keeping control of the weight and maintaining the same elbow angle and shoulder position.
Complete desired reps and return the lever to the safety/rest position before releasing the handles; avoid using momentum or excessive shoulder elevation during the set.
exercises.description: Seated on an incline lever press machine using a neutral (hammer) grip, this exercise drives the handles upward to target the upper chest. It emphasizes the upper pectorals while also engaging the anterior delts and triceps for pressing and stabilization.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Chest Press
exercises.notes: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
instructions: Set the pulleys to chest height and attach single-handle grips to each side.
Stand facing away from the machine and grab a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip; bring the handles to chest level with elbows bent and hands near the sternum.
Step forward into a staggered or shoulder-width stance so one foot is slightly in front of the other; brace your core and keep a slight bend in the knees.
Keep shoulders down and back, chest up, and press both handles forward and inward until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press; hold the end position briefly while keeping tension on the cables.
Inhale and slowly control the return of the handles back to the starting position at chest level, allowing the shoulder blades to set but not excessively retract.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a stable torso and avoiding leaning forward or using excessive lower-back arching.
exercises.description: A standing cable press that targets the chest by pressing handles forward from chest level against cable resistance; the movement also engages the anterior delts and triceps while the core stabilizes the torso. Performed facing away from the cable column with a staggered stance for balance.

exercises.name: Dumbbell 2 Point Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back and shoulders supported and one foot planted firmly on the floor near the bench.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back and shoulders supported and one foot planted firmly on the floor near the bench.

Bend the opposite knee and lift that foot off the floor so only one foot is providing support (single‑leg position); press your hips up into a bridge so your torso forms a straight line from shoulder to knee and brace your abs.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand (or a single dumbbell if using one) at chest level with a neutral grip or palms facing forward, elbows bent and tucked at about a 45° angle to your torso.
Inhale, then press the weight straight up by extending your arms until elbows are nearly locked and the dumbbells are over your chest while keeping hips elevated and core tight.
Pause briefly at the top, keeping shoulders stable on the bench and hips high, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to your chest under control while maintaining the bridge position.
Complete the prescribed reps on one leg, then carefully lower your hips, switch legs, and repeat on the other side. Keep movement controlled and avoid hyperextending the lower back.
exercises.description: A bench press performed while holding a hip bridge (single-leg position) to add core and glute stability demand; you press the dumbbells from chest to full arm extension while maintaining an elevated hip. This combines chest pressing with unilateral lower‑body stabilization.

exercises.name: Cable Palm Rotational Row
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a low or mid cable pulley.
instructions: Set a single handle on a low or mid cable pulley. Select a weight you can control for the full range of motion.
Stand facing the machine with feet about hip‑width apart, knees slightly bent. Hinge forward at the hips so your torso is angled forward (approximately 30–45°), maintaining a neutral spine.
Reach forward and grasp the handle with the working hand; start with the arm extended and the palm in the starting orientation (e.g., palm down or neutral). Brace your core to prevent torso rotation.
Initiate the row by pulling the handle toward your torso while simultaneously rotating your palm (rotate the wrist/forearm) so the hand ends in the inward/neutral position as you finish the pull.
Keep the elbow close to the body and lead the motion with the elbow and shoulder blade—squeeze the shoulder blades together at the peak contraction to target the upper back.
Hold the contracted position briefly (1–2 seconds), then slowly extend the arm forward while reversing the palm rotation back to the start, controlling the cable on the return.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Maintain a stable, hip‑hinged standing position throughout and avoid using torso momentum or excessive lower back arch.
exercises.description: A single‑arm cable row performed from a standing, hip‑hinged position where the hand rotates (palm rotation) during the pull to emphasize the upper back and rear shoulder musculature. The movement combines a horizontal rowing action with forearm/palm rotation to increase upper‑back and rear‑delt engagement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

workout.title: Chest/Back - BODYBUILD/ENDURANCE
workout.description: Chest/Back workout focusing on bodybuild and endurance training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Chest Dip
exercises.notes: Starting position: Stand between parallel dip bars or on a dip station and grasp the handles with a firm neutral or slightly pronated grip; support your weight with straight arms and feet off the ground (legs can be bent at the knees or crossed behind you).
instructions: Starting position: Stand between parallel dip bars or on a dip station and grasp the handles with a firm neutral or slightly pronated grip; support your weight with straight arms and feet off the ground (legs can be bent at the knees or crossed behind you).
Adopt a slight forward lean from the hips so your chest moves ahead of your hands; keep your core braced and spine neutral throughout the movement.
Begin the descent by bending at the elbows and lowering your torso in a controlled manner, allowing your elbows to track slightly outward (about 30–45° from the torso) to place emphasis on the chest.
Lower until your shoulders are at or just below elbow level or until you feel a full but comfortable stretch in the chest; avoid excessive depth that causes shoulder pain.
Push back up by driving through the palms and extending the elbows while maintaining the forward lean and chest-up posture; exhale as you press upward.
Return to the start with arms nearly straight but avoid aggressively locking the elbows at the top; maintain control and avoid swinging the body.
Perform repetitions with a steady tempo (e.g., 2–3 seconds down, 1 second pause, 1 second up) and focus on chest contraction each rep.

Progression and safety: if bodyweight is too difficult use assisted dip machine or band assistance; if you want more chest emphasis increase forward lean slightly. Stop if you experience sharp shoulder pain.
exercises.description: A bodyweight dip performed on parallel bars with a forward lean to emphasize the chest; the movement lowers the torso by bending the elbows then pushes the body back up to full arm extension. It targets the pectorals while the triceps and anterior delts assist and stabilize.

exercises.name: Barbell Pullover To Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and hips down (bench‑supported supine position); hold a barbell with a double overhand grip roughly shoulder‑width apart.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and hips down (bench‑supported supine position); hold a barbell with a double overhand grip roughly shoulder‑width apart.
Start with the barbell held vertically over your chest with elbows slightly bent and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.

Inhale and slowly lower the barbell in an arc behind your head by flexing at the shoulders, keeping the upper arms angled and elbows soft until you feel a stretch across the lats/pectorals (do not let the lower back over-arch).
Pause briefly at the bottom of the pullover stretch while maintaining core tension and controlled breathing.
Drive the barbell back in a controlled arc over the chest by contracting the lats and pectorals; as the bar reaches chest level, transition into the press.
Exhale and press the barbell straight up until arms are fully extended overhead, using shoulders and triceps to finish the movement while keeping the ribs down and abs engaged.
Slowly lower the barbell back down to the chest under control to reset for the next rep, maintaining a steady rhythm and avoiding excessive momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions. Use a manageable weight to protect the shoulders and maintain proper form throughout.
exercises.description: A supine combination movement that begins as a barbell pullover (bringing the weight in an arc behind the head to stretch and work the lats) and finishes as an overhead press, adding shoulder and triceps work. It trains lat length and upper‑body pressing strength while requiring core stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips and push your butt back until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (slight knee bend), keeping the spine neutral and eyes looking a few feet ahead.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or pronated grip.
Hinge at the hips and push your butt back until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (slight knee bend), keeping the spine neutral and eyes looking a few feet ahead.
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders with weights beneath you; brace your core to prevent rounding of the lower back.
Pull the dumbbells toward your lower ribcage or waist by driving your elbows up and back, squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a tight upper back, then lower the weights slowly under control until the arms are extended.
Repeat for the desired reps while keeping the torso still—avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation.
Breathe out as you row the weights up and inhale as you lower them; stop the set if you cannot maintain a neutral spine.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell row is a standing, hip-hinged pulling exercise that targets the muscles of the upper back while also engaging the lats and arms. It develops horizontal pulling strength and scapular retraction while the spine and forearms stabilize the position.

exercises.name: Cable Single Arm High Row
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a high cable pulley.
instructions: Set a single handle on a high cable pulley. Adjust a bench or chest pad so your chest is firmly supported and you can sit upright without leaning forward or rotating.
Sit with feet flat on the floor, chest pressed into the support, and grab the handle with a neutral or overhand grip using one arm; start with the arm extended and shoulder slightly protracted.
Brace your core, keep your torso stationary against the chest support, and retract your shoulder blade slightly before initiating the pull.
Pull the handle down and back in a smooth arc by driving your elbow up and back toward your ribcage, focusing on squeezing the upper back and rear delt at the end range.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction while maintaining a neutral wrist and no torso rotation or shrugging, then slowly extend the arm back to the start position under control.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Maintain steady breathing (exhale during the pull, inhale during the return) and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: A single-arm high cable row performed with chest support to isolate the upper back while minimizing torso movement. The movement pulls a high-set handle down and back with the elbow driving toward the ribcage to emphasize the upper back and rear delts.

exercises.name: Cable Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Set two cable pulleys at a low or mid position (roughly at or slightly below chest height when standing) and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set two cable pulleys at a low or mid position (roughly at or slightly below chest height when standing) and attach single-handle grips.
Place a flat bench centered between the cable towers and lie supine (on your back) with feet flat on the floor and head supported by the bench.
Reach up and grab one handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-facing grip; start with the arms out to the sides and elbows slightly bent (soft elbows).
Retract and stabilize your shoulder blades on the bench to minimize shoulder elevation and protect the spine.
Exhale and bring one hand (or alternate hands) in a smooth arc toward the center of your chest, maintaining the same slight elbow bend and keeping the motion driven by the pectorals.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction with the hands meeting or slightly overlapping at midline, squeezing the chest.
Inhale and slowly return the working arm to the start position under control, resisting the cable's pull; keep torso and hips stable and avoid excessive arching of the lower back.
Repeat for the desired reps, alternating arms if performing the alternating variation; keep tempo controlled and avoid using momentum.
exercises.description: A supine cable fly performed on a flat bench where each arm works alternately to bring the handle across the chest, emphasizing horizontal adduction of the shoulder to target the pectorals. The exercise uses a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo to isolate the chest.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
