exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back (supine on an incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability; bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly (pinch the shoulder blades together) to stabilize the chest and protect the shoulders.
Begin the repetition by slowly lowering the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout; lower until you feel a comfortable stretch in the upper chest.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid dropping the weights below the plane of the chest or allowing the elbows to fully lock or flare excessively.
Drive the dumbbells back up by horizontally adducting the shoulders (bringing the arms together) until they meet or nearly meet above the sternum; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then inhale and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a neutral neck and avoid shrugging the shoulders; use a weight that allows strict form and full control through both eccentric and concentric phases.
exercises.description: Performed lying back on an inclined bench, the dumbbell incline fly isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from open to together while maintaining a slight elbow bend. It emphasizes pectoral horizontal adduction with shoulder stabilization from the delts and grip support from the forearms.

exercises.name: Cable Reverse Grip Pulldown
exercises.notes: Attach a straight bar and grasp it with a reverse (underhand, palms facing you) grip about shoulder-width or slightly narrower.
instructions: Sit on the lat pulldown bench with feet flat, knees secured under the thigh pads and torso upright (seated position).
Attach a straight bar and grasp it with a reverse (underhand, palms facing you) grip about shoulder-width or slightly narrower.
Start with arms fully extended overhead, shoulders relaxed and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Exhale and pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back, bringing the bar to the upper chest while keeping the scapulae down and squeezing the lats.
Pause briefly at the bottom to feel the contraction in the lats and biceps; avoid excessive backward lean.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with controlled movement until arms are fully extended.
Keep wrists neutral and avoid using momentum or swinging the torso; reduce weight if hips or torso begin to move.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a reverse (underhand) grip that emphasizes the lats while placing extra demand on the biceps and forearms. Pull the bar down to the upper chest with elbows driving down and back while keeping the torso stable.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with shoulders and head supported, feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the hips.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with shoulders and head supported, feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the hips.
Grasp one dumbbell by the ends with both hands (or hold a single DB handle with hands cupped), arms extended above chest with a slight bend in the elbows; maintain a neutral spine and braced core.

Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head, keeping the same slight elbow bend and focusing on stretching the lats and chest; stop when you feel a comfortable stretch or when the dumbbell is roughly level with your forehead/bench line.
Pause briefly at the bottom without letting the lower back over-arch; keep hips down and core engaged to prevent excessive lumbar extension.
Exhale and pull the dumbbell back in the same arcing path until it is over your chest, initiating the movement by contracting the lats and upper back rather than forcing it with the arms.
Maintain steady, controlled tempo for each rep, avoid jerking or using momentum, and keep elbow angle constant throughout the movement.
Adjust range of motion or weight if you cannot maintain scapular control or if you feel pain in the shoulders; perform 8–15 controlled reps depending on your goal.
exercises.description: Supine dumbbell pullover performed on a flat bench: the weight is moved in an arc from over the chest to behind the head and back, emphasizing a stretch and contraction of the lats while also engaging the chest and triceps.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back (supine on an incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability; bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly (pinch the shoulder blades together) to stabilize the chest and protect the shoulders.
Begin the repetition by slowly lowering the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout; lower until you feel a comfortable stretch in the upper chest.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid dropping the weights below the plane of the chest or allowing the elbows to fully lock or flare excessively.
Drive the dumbbells back up by horizontally adducting the shoulders (bringing the arms together) until they meet or nearly meet above the sternum; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then inhale and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a neutral neck and avoid shrugging the shoulders; use a weight that allows strict form and full control through both eccentric and concentric phases.
exercises.description: Performed lying back on an inclined bench, the dumbbell incline fly isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from open to together while maintaining a slight elbow bend. It emphasizes pectoral horizontal adduction with shoulder stabilization from the delts and grip support from the forearms.

exercises.name: Dumbbell One Arm Row (rack support)
exercises.notes: Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor while keeping a slight bend in both knees; maintain a neutral spine and long neck throughout.
instructions: Stand with a staggered stance (one foot forward, one foot back) beside a rack; place the hand nearest the rack on the rail at about hip height for support.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor while keeping a slight bend in both knees; maintain a neutral spine and long neck throughout.
Let the working arm hang straight down holding a dumbbell with a neutral or overhand grip, shoulder relaxed.
Brace your core and pull the dumbbell upward in a vertical path, leading with the elbow and driving it toward your ribcage/hip; keep the elbow close to your body.
Squeeze the shoulder blade toward the spine at the top of the movement and pause briefly to maximize lat/upper-back contraction.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position under control until the arm is fully extended, maintaining torso stability and avoiding rotation.
Complete the desired reps on one side, then switch sides so the opposite hand supports on the rack and repeat. Keep breathing steadily (exhale on the pull, inhale on the return).
exercises.description: A single-arm bent-over row performed with one hand bracing on a rack for support; the exercise emphasizes a unilateral pull to develop the lats while also engaging the upper back and arm muscles. The support hand and staggered stance increase stability and allow focus on a full, controlled contraction of the pulling side.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.

exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Reverse Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is leaned forward (torso roughly parallel or at a 30–45° angle depending on flexibility), keeping the spine neutral and neck aligned.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a reverse (palms-up/supinated) grip.

Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is leaned forward (torso roughly parallel or at a 30–45° angle depending on flexibility), keeping the spine neutral and neck aligned.
Let the arms hang straight toward the floor with the dumbbells under your shoulders; brace your abs to stabilize the spine.
Initiate the row by pulling the dumbbells up and back toward your lower ribs/waist, driving the elbows close to your body and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top with scapular retraction, keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive back extension or shrugging.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control until the arms are nearly extended, maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out as you pull and inhaling as you lower.
exercises.description: A bent-over reverse-grip dumbbell row performed from a hip-hinged position to emphasize the upper back and rear shoulder muscles while using a supinated (reverse) grip. The movement pulls the weights toward the torso, engaging the scapular retractors and arm flexors.

exercises.name: Lever Supinated Grip Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load the desired plates and set the chest pad/seat so your chest rests against the pad with arms fully extended when you reach the handles; sit upright with feet braced on the platform or footrests.
instructions: Load the desired plates and set the chest pad/seat so your chest rests against the pad with arms fully extended when you reach the handles; sit upright with feet braced on the platform or footrests.
Grasp the handles with a supinated (palms-up/underhand) grip, wrists neutral and shoulder blades relaxed forward at the start.
Brace your core, keep your chest on the pad and maintain a neutral spine (no excessive arching or leaning forward).
Exhale and pull the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows straight back—lead with the elbows rather than the hands.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together and feel the contraction in your lats and upper back at the end range; keep the wrists and forearms steady.
Pause for a brief second at peak contraction while maintaining an upright, chest-supported position.
Inhale and slowly extend your arms back to the starting position with controlled movement until your elbows are almost straight, keeping tension on the muscles.
Repeat for the prescribed reps; avoid using torso momentum or excessive shoulder elevation—focus on controlled scapular retraction and full range.
exercises.description: Seated row performed on a plate-loaded lever machine using a supinated (underhand) grip; the movement pulls the handles toward the torso to drive horizontal shoulder extension and scapular retraction. It emphasizes the lats while also loading the arms and upper back musculature.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or place your forearms against the pads with a slight bend in the elbows; start with arms out to the sides and shoulder blades pulled back and down.
Exhale and drive the handles forward and together in a smooth arc by contracting the pectorals, keeping the elbows slightly bent and wrists neutral.
Pause briefly when the hands/handles meet or are directly in front of your chest, feeling the squeeze in the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly allow the levers to return to the start position under control, maintaining shoulder blade stability and avoiding rapid letting go or trunk leaning.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; use a weight that allows full control of both concentric and eccentric phases without excessive shoulder or back movement.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly performed on a lever/pec-deck: you bring the arms together in front of the chest against resistance to isolate the pectoral muscles. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and controlled arc to emphasize the inner chest.

exercises.name: Cable Pushdown (straight arm)
exercises.notes: Attach a straight bar, rope, or single handle to a high pulley.
instructions: Attach a straight bar, rope, or single handle to a high pulley. Select a moderate weight that allows controlled movement while keeping arms mostly straight.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart (or a slight stagger for balance). Grasp the handle with both hands (pronated or neutral grip) and step back slightly so the cable is taut.

Hinge slightly forward at the hips so your torso leans about 10–20 degrees; keep knees soft and maintain a neutral spine. Arms should be extended in front of you with a small, fixed bend in the elbows (not fully locked).

Initiate the rep by driving the shoulders into extension: press the hands downward and back in a controlled arc until the hands reach thigh level and you feel maximal contraction in the lats. Keep the elbows stationary and avoid bending them to pull.
Pause briefly at the bottom while squeezing the lats and maintaining straight-arm tension.
Slowly return the handle to the starting position under control, keeping constant tension on the cable and avoiding letting the shoulders fully relax at the top.
Breathe out during the downward (working) phase and inhale while returning to the start. Perform desired number of reps with steady tempo.
exercises.description: Standing straight-arm cable pushdowns target the lats by driving the arms down from a high cable with minimal elbow flexion. The movement emphasizes shoulder extension and scapular retraction while keeping the torso stable.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Pendlay Row
exercises.notes: Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (hip hinge), knees slightly bent.
Grip the bar with a double overhand or mixed grip about shoulder-width apart, keep a neutral spine and eyes looking a few feet ahead on the floor.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades down and back, then explosively row the bar in a straight line to your lower chest/upper abdomen, driving your elbows back.
Squeeze your upper back at the top for 1 second while keeping the torso stable (no rowing with the hips or excessive torso rotation).
Lower the bar under control all the way back to the floor so it comes to a complete stop, reset any slack, then repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a flat back and braced core throughout; if your lower back rounds or you must use momentum, reduce weight or raise the bar height.
exercises.description: The Barbell Pendlay Row is a strict horizontal barbell row performed from a dead stop on the floor each rep, emphasizing the upper back and lats while minimizing torso momentum. It uses a hip hinge with a flat back and pulls the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen before returning it to the floor.

exercises.name: Cable Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single-handle attachment on a cable machine to a high pulley (above shoulder height).
instructions: Set a single-handle attachment on a cable machine to a high pulley (above shoulder height).
Stand facing across from the pulley in a staggered stance (one foot forward for balance) with a slight forward lean at the hips; engage your core and keep a neutral spine.
Reach up and grab the handle with the working hand using a neutral grip; allow the arm to start out to the side with a small bend in the elbow.
Depress and stabilize your shoulder (avoid shrugging) and pull the handle down and across your body in a smooth arc toward the opposite mid-chest, rotating the torso slightly as needed.
At the end of the movement, actively squeeze the pectorals and serratus anterior while maintaining the elbow bend; pause briefly at peak contraction.
Slowly and under control, return the handle to the starting position allowing the chest to stretch but keeping tension on the cable; do not let the weight stack slam.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Keep breathing steadily (exhale as you pull across, inhale as you return).
Maintain a slight elbow bend throughout, avoid shoulder hyperextension, and do not use excessive torso rotation or momentum to drive the movement.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable crossover isolates one side of the chest by pulling a cable across the body from a high pulley, emphasizing the pectoral contraction and allowing a strong end-range squeeze. It is performed standing with a slight forward lean and a controlled return to the start position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back supported and feet flat on the floor for stability.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back supported and feet flat on the floor for stability.
Hold one dumbbell with both hands (palms cupped against the underside of the top plate) and extend your arms straight over your chest with a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core and keep your ribs down to maintain a neutral spine; do not hyperextend the lower back.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head by allowing your shoulders to extend, keeping the elbows slightly bent and fixed at that angle.
Lower until you feel a stretch in the lats/upper chest or until your range allows without shoulder discomfort.
Exhale and pull the dumbbell back in the same arc to the starting position over your chest by contracting the lats and driving the elbows down toward your hips.
Pause briefly at the top, maintain control on every rep, and avoid jerking the weight or flaring the ribs upward.
Perform repetitions with a controlled tempo; reduce weight if you cannot maintain a stable core and neutral spine.
exercises.description: Supine dumbbell pullover performed on a flat bench involves lowering a single dumbbell in an arc from over the chest behind the head to stretch and load the latissimus and then pulling it back over the chest. It emphasizes lat lengthening and shoulder extension while also engaging the chest, triceps and serratus anterior for control.

exercises.name: Close Grip Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Grasp a pull-up bar with an underhand (supinated) close grip — hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower, thumbs wrapped around the bar.
instructions: Grasp a pull-up bar with an underhand (supinated) close grip — hands about shoulder-width or slightly narrower, thumbs wrapped around the bar.

Start from a dead hang with arms fully extended, shoulders relaxed, torso vertical, legs either straight or lightly crossed at the ankles; brace your core and squeeze the glutes to prevent excessive swinging.

Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blades and driving the elbows down and back while pulling your chest toward the bar; focus on contracting the lats and pulling with the elbows rather than just the hands.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or your chest reaches the bar, keeping the torso fairly upright and avoiding kipping or excessive leg drive.
Pause briefly at the top under control, then slowly lower yourself back to a full dead hang by extending the arms and allowing the shoulder blades to protract and depress.
Repeat for the desired number of reps. Breathe out while pulling up and inhale while lowering. Maintain strict form and avoid momentum to maximize lat and biceps engagement.
exercises.description: A close-grip chin-up is a vertical pulling exercise performed from a dead hang with a narrow, underhand (supinated) grip that emphasizes the lats while placing increased demand on the biceps and forearms. It builds upper-back strength and arm pulling power.

exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.

Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Pullover To Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and hips down (bench‑supported supine position); hold a barbell with a double overhand grip roughly shoulder‑width apart.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and hips down (bench‑supported supine position); hold a barbell with a double overhand grip roughly shoulder‑width apart.
Start with the barbell held vertically over your chest with elbows slightly bent and core braced to maintain a neutral spine.

Inhale and slowly lower the barbell in an arc behind your head by flexing at the shoulders, keeping the upper arms angled and elbows soft until you feel a stretch across the lats/pectorals (do not let the lower back over-arch).
Pause briefly at the bottom of the pullover stretch while maintaining core tension and controlled breathing.
Drive the barbell back in a controlled arc over the chest by contracting the lats and pectorals; as the bar reaches chest level, transition into the press.
Exhale and press the barbell straight up until arms are fully extended overhead, using shoulders and triceps to finish the movement while keeping the ribs down and abs engaged.
Slowly lower the barbell back down to the chest under control to reset for the next rep, maintaining a steady rhythm and avoiding excessive momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions. Use a manageable weight to protect the shoulders and maintain proper form throughout.
exercises.description: A supine combination movement that begins as a barbell pullover (bringing the weight in an arc behind the head to stretch and work the lats) and finishes as an overhead press, adding shoulder and triceps work. It trains lat length and upper‑body pressing strength while requiring core stabilization.

exercises.name: Lever One Arm Lateral High Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine seat and chest/back pad so the handle is reachable with your arm extended overhead while seated; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine seat and chest/back pad so the handle is reachable with your arm extended overhead while seated; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your chest braced against the pad, feet flat on the floor, core engaged and torso stable to prevent twisting.
Reach up and grasp the single high handle with one hand (neutral or pronated grip) while keeping the shoulder down and away from the ear.
Initiate the movement by driving the elbow down and back in a lateral arc, leading with the elbow so the hand moves toward the side of your ribs and the shoulder blade retracts and rotates downward.
Squeeze the upper back (scapulae) at the end range for 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive shoulder elevation or rotation.
Slowly return the handle to the start position with control, fully extending the arm while maintaining tension in the working muscles.
Perform the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat. Maintain steady breathing (exhale while pulling, inhale while returning).
exercises.description: A single-arm high pulley/lever row performed from a seated position that pulls the handle laterally and upward to emphasize the upper back and rear shoulder musculature. It focuses on scapular retraction and elbow-led pulling to target the upper back and lats while the arm and grip work the biceps and forearms.

exercises.name: Assisted Pull-up
exercises.notes: Set the assistance weight on the machine so you can complete the desired reps with good form.
instructions: Set the assistance weight on the machine so you can complete the desired reps with good form.
Kneel on the machine's knee pad (or place knees on the assistance platform) and reach up to grasp the pull-up bar with a shoulder-width, pronated (overhand) grip; keep head neutral.
Before pulling, pull your shoulder blades down and back (scapular retraction) to engage the lats and upper back.
Pull your chest upward toward the bar by driving the elbows down and back, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging.
Pause briefly at the top with your chin over the bar, maintaining scapular retraction.
Slowly lower yourself back to the starting position until your arms are fully extended, maintaining control throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the prescribed number of repetitions, and adjust assistance if reps become too easy or too difficult.
exercises.description: Kneeling on an assisted pull-up machine, the exerciser pulls their torso up toward an overhead bar using a counterweight to reduce load; this movement primarily trains the latissimus dorsi and upper back pulling muscles. Control the descent to emphasize the eccentric phase.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying One Arm Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand at chest level with a firm grip (palm facing forward or neutral); place your free hand on your chest or the bench for balance.
Retract your shoulder blade slightly on the working side and brace your core to prevent your torso from rotating.
Exhale and press the dumbbell straight up until the arm is nearly extended, keeping a soft bend in the elbow (do not fully lock out).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell under control back to the chest level, keeping the elbow at about a 45-degree angle relative to the torso.
Maintain steady posture and avoid twisting; reset your scapula and core between reps if needed.
Complete the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat with equal control and setup.
exercises.description: A single-arm dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a flat bench to train the pectorals unilaterally while the triceps and delts assist; the opposite hand rests for stability. Emphasizes controlled pressing and core stabilization to prevent torso rotation.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Straight Arm Pullover
exercises.notes: Brace your core and keep your hips stable (avoid excessive lower-back arch); inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head until you feel a deep stretch in the lats/upper chest or your range limit.
instructions: Position yourself supine on a flat bench with your upper back and shoulders supported, feet flat on the floor and knees bent; hold one dumbbell with both hands (palms under one end) above your chest with arms nearly straight and a slight bend in the elbows.

Brace your core and keep your hips stable (avoid excessive lower-back arch); inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head until you feel a deep stretch in the lats/upper chest or your range limit.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining the slight elbow bend and scapular stability (do not shrug or let the shoulders round forward).
Exhale and pull the dumbbell back in the same arc to the start position above your chest by driving the motion with the lats and ribcage expansion, keeping the arms mostly straight.
Repeat for the desired reps with slow, controlled tempo; avoid using momentum, excessive elbow bend, or letting the lower back hyperextend.
exercises.description: A supine dumbbell pullover performed with mostly straight arms that moves the weight in an arc from over the chest to behind the head, emphasizing a long-lever stretch and concentric pull to work the lats and upper chest.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Reverse Grip Incline Bench Row
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and position it so your chest rests firmly on the pad with feet braced on the floor or foot supports (prone position).
Load a barbell and grasp it with a shoulder-width reverse (underhand) grip so palms face up and arms hang straight down toward the floor.
Start with your chest pressed into the bench, shoulders relaxed and scapulae slightly protracted, core engaged and neck neutral.

Initiate the movement by retracting your scapulae and pulling the barbell upward in a controlled path toward your lower chest / upper abdomen, leading with the elbows and keeping them close to your sides.
Pause 1 second at the top and squeeze the lats and upper back without hyperextending the spine or lifting your chest off the bench.
Lower the barbell slowly back to the start with full arm extension while maintaining tension in the lats; avoid dropping the weight or using momentum.
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out while pulling and inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: Prone incline bench row performed with a reverse (underhand) grip that emphasizes the lats while also engaging the upper back and arms. Lying chest-down on an incline bench, you pull a barbell up toward the lower chest/upper abdomen and lower it under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping the torso fixed (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation) and continue to breathe: exhale while pulling, inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with dumbbells where you hinge at the hips and pull the weights toward your torso, emphasizing the lats and upper back. The movement also engages the arms and posterior chain to stabilize the hinged position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Reverse Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is leaned forward (torso roughly parallel or at a 30–45° angle depending on flexibility), keeping the spine neutral and neck aligned.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a reverse (palms-up/supinated) grip.

Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is leaned forward (torso roughly parallel or at a 30–45° angle depending on flexibility), keeping the spine neutral and neck aligned.
Let the arms hang straight toward the floor with the dumbbells under your shoulders; brace your abs to stabilize the spine.
Initiate the row by pulling the dumbbells up and back toward your lower ribs/waist, driving the elbows close to your body and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top with scapular retraction, keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive back extension or shrugging.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control until the arms are nearly extended, maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out as you pull and inhaling as you lower.
exercises.description: A bent-over reverse-grip dumbbell row performed from a hip-hinged position to emphasize the upper back and rear shoulder muscles while using a supinated (reverse) grip. The movement pulls the weights toward the torso, engaging the scapular retractors and arm flexors.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Scapula Row
exercises.notes: Set up with one knee and the same-side hand resting on a flat bench (kneeling on bench), the opposite foot planted on the floor for stability; grip a dumbbell in the free hand with the arm hanging straight toward the floor.
instructions: Set up with one knee and the same-side hand resting on a flat bench (kneeling on bench), the opposite foot planted on the floor for stability; grip a dumbbell in the free hand with the arm hanging straight toward the floor.
Hinge forward at the hips so your torso is roughly parallel to the floor while keeping a neutral spine and bracing your core; maintain a slight bend in the supporting knee.

Begin the movement by retracting and depressing the scapula of the working side (pull the shoulder blade back and down) while keeping the elbow nearly straight or allowing only a small bend; do not initiate the movement by bending the elbow.
Squeeze the shoulder blades together at the top for 1 second, feeling the contraction across the upper back and between the shoulder blades.
Slowly allow the scapula to protract and return the arm to the fully extended starting position in a controlled manner.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides so the previously supporting hand/knee becomes the working side. Maintain neutral spine and avoid torso rotation throughout.
exercises.description: A bent-over, bench-supported dumbbell row that emphasizes scapular retraction to target the upper back and mid-trapezius/rhomboid area. Performed from a kneeling/bent-over position with a single-arm dumbbell, focusing on pulling the shoulder blade back and down.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Straight Arm Pullover
exercises.notes: Brace your core and keep your hips stable (avoid excessive lower-back arch); inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head until you feel a deep stretch in the lats/upper chest or your range limit.
instructions: Position yourself supine on a flat bench with your upper back and shoulders supported, feet flat on the floor and knees bent; hold one dumbbell with both hands (palms under one end) above your chest with arms nearly straight and a slight bend in the elbows.

Brace your core and keep your hips stable (avoid excessive lower-back arch); inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head until you feel a deep stretch in the lats/upper chest or your range limit.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining the slight elbow bend and scapular stability (do not shrug or let the shoulders round forward).
Exhale and pull the dumbbell back in the same arc to the start position above your chest by driving the motion with the lats and ribcage expansion, keeping the arms mostly straight.
Repeat for the desired reps with slow, controlled tempo; avoid using momentum, excessive elbow bend, or letting the lower back hyperextend.
exercises.description: A supine dumbbell pullover performed with mostly straight arms that moves the weight in an arc from over the chest to behind the head, emphasizing a long-lever stretch and concentric pull to work the lats and upper chest.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

workout.title: Chest/Back - BODYBUILD/ENDURANCE
workout.description: Chest/Back workout focusing on bodybuild and endurance training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a supine pressing exercise performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also working the triceps and anterior delts. The lifter lowers a barbell to the chest and presses it back to full arm extension under control.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back.
instructions: Sit on the end of a flat bench holding a dumbbell in each hand at your thighs.

Lie back (supine) on the bench while bringing the dumbbells to chest level; plant your feet flat on the floor and create a slight natural arch in your lower back. Retract your shoulder blades and keep them contacting the bench.
Start with the dumbbells at mid-chest, elbows bent about 90 degrees and wrists neutral (dumbbell handles aligned with forearms).
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly straight but not hyperextended; keep wrists stacked over elbows.

Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position, maintaining control and stopping when the dumbbells reach chest level or your elbows form ~90 degrees. Keep elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from your torso to protect the shoulders.
Keep your core braced and feet firmly planted for stability throughout each rep; avoid excessive flaring of the ribs or letting the shoulders lift off the bench.
Use a spotter for heavy loads and choose a weight that allows controlled full-range reps with good form.
exercises.description: Flat dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a bench, pressing two dumbbells upward from chest level to develop the pectorals; the movement also recruits the anterior delts and triceps for stability and lockout.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45 degrees and place a pair of dumbbells nearby.
Sit on the bench with the dumbbells resting on your thighs, feet flat on the floor for stability.
Use your legs to help lift the dumbbells as you lie back so the weights are beside your upper chest; hold them with a neutral or slightly supinated grip.
Retract your shoulder blades and press your upper back into the bench so your chest is proud (maintain a stable supine position on the incline).
Plant your feet and brace your core to avoid excessive arching of the spine.
Press the dumbbells upward and slightly together in a controlled path until your arms are almost fully extended (do not lock the elbows).
Exhale as you press up, pause briefly at the top with control, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position at the upper chest level.
Keep the elbows at a moderate angle (not flared excessively) and the wrists neutral throughout the movement.
Repeat for desired repetitions and use a spotter for heavy sets or if you cannot safely return the weights to your thighs/stand them up.
exercises.description: The dumbbell incline bench press is performed lying supine on an incline bench and pressing dumbbells upward to emphasize the upper portion of the chest. It builds strength and mass in the upper pectorals while also recruiting the front delts and triceps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Single Arm Row
exercises.notes: Keep your spine neutral, neck in line with the spine, and the non‑working hand resting on your thigh or a bench for support if needed.
instructions: Stand with feet hip‑width apart holding a dumbbell in one hand; bend the knees slightly and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent‑over position).
Keep your spine neutral, neck in line with the spine, and the non‑working hand resting on your thigh or a bench for support if needed.
Let the working arm hang straight down from the shoulder with the palm facing in (neutral grip).
Brace your core and pull the dumbbell straight up toward your hip/waist by driving the elbow back and squeezing the shoulder blade toward the spine; keep the elbow close to the body.
Pause briefly at the top with the scapula retracted, then lower the weight under control until the arm is fully extended.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Maintain the hip hinge and avoid rotating the torso or using momentum to lift the weight.
exercises.description: A single‑arm bent‑over row is a standing hip‑hinge rowing movement that pulls a dumbbell toward the hip to develop the lats and upper back while also working the biceps and forearms. It is performed from a bent‑over position with a neutral spine and controlled eccentric lowering.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back (supine on an incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability; bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly (pinch the shoulder blades together) to stabilize the chest and protect the shoulders.
Begin the repetition by slowly lowering the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout; lower until you feel a comfortable stretch in the upper chest.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid dropping the weights below the plane of the chest or allowing the elbows to fully lock or flare excessively.
Drive the dumbbells back up by horizontally adducting the shoulders (bringing the arms together) until they meet or nearly meet above the sternum; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then inhale and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a neutral neck and avoid shrugging the shoulders; use a weight that allows strict form and full control through both eccentric and concentric phases.
exercises.description: Performed lying back on an inclined bench, the dumbbell incline fly isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from open to together while maintaining a slight elbow bend. It emphasizes pectoral horizontal adduction with shoulder stabilization from the delts and grip support from the forearms.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back supported and feet flat on the floor for stability.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your upper back supported and feet flat on the floor for stability.
Hold one dumbbell with both hands (palms cupped against the underside of the top plate) and extend your arms straight over your chest with a slight bend in the elbows.
Brace your core and keep your ribs down to maintain a neutral spine; do not hyperextend the lower back.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell in a controlled arc behind your head by allowing your shoulders to extend, keeping the elbows slightly bent and fixed at that angle.
Lower until you feel a stretch in the lats/upper chest or until your range allows without shoulder discomfort.
Exhale and pull the dumbbell back in the same arc to the starting position over your chest by contracting the lats and driving the elbows down toward your hips.
Pause briefly at the top, maintain control on every rep, and avoid jerking the weight or flaring the ribs upward.
Perform repetitions with a controlled tempo; reduce weight if you cannot maintain a stable core and neutral spine.
exercises.description: Supine dumbbell pullover performed on a flat bench involves lowering a single dumbbell in an arc from over the chest behind the head to stretch and load the latissimus and then pulling it back over the chest. It emphasizes lat lengthening and shoulder extension while also engaging the chest, triceps and serratus anterior for control.

exercises.name: Cable Wide Grip Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the wide bar with a pronated (overhand) grip wider than shoulder width.
Sit tall with a slight natural arch in the lower back, feet flat, chest up and head neutral.
Begin the rep by inhaling, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back toward your ribcage.
Lead the motion with your elbows and squeeze your shoulder blades together at the bottom, bringing the bar to the upper chest or collarbone level.
Pause briefly while maintaining tension in the lats, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive backward lean or swinging.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with controlled extension of the arms until you feel a full stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining steady tempo and controlled breathing throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip lat pulldown using an overhand wide bar to pull the cable down toward the upper chest, emphasizing shoulder adduction and scapular depression to work the latissimus dorsi. Performed seated with knees braced to isolate the back and minimize leg drive.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
