workout.title: Chest/Back - BODYBUILD/ENDURANCE
workout.description: Chest/Back workout focusing on bodybuild and endurance training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Lever Low Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Load the desired plates on the machine and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, arms extended forward at the start position.
instructions: Sit on the seat with your chest pressed against the pad (chest-supported), feet flat on the footrests or floor and knees slightly bent.
Load the desired plates on the machine and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, arms extended forward at the start position.
Pull your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction) to initiate the movement, then drive your elbows straight back, keeping them close to your sides.
Continue pulling until the handles reach your torso or the elbows are behind the body, squeezing the lats and upper back at the top for a 1-2 second pause.
Slowly return the handles to the start position under control by extending the arms and allowing the shoulder blades to protract, keeping tension on the lats throughout.
Keep your torso stable and chest on the pad the entire set — avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum to complete reps.
Breathe out during the pull (concentric) and inhale as you return (eccentric); choose a weight that allows full range of motion with good form.
exercises.description: A seated, plate-loaded low row variation that targets the latissimus dorsi with emphasis on mid-back scapular retraction; performed from a chest-supported seated position using the machine's lever handles. Focuses on a controlled horizontal pulling motion to develop back thickness and arm pulling strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Decline Hammer Press
exercises.notes: Set the bench to a decline angle and place your feet securely under the foot pads so you are stable and supine on the bench.
instructions: Set the bench to a decline angle and place your feet securely under the foot pads so you are stable and supine on the bench.

Pick up two dumbbells and sit at the edge of the bench; with a neutral (hammer) grip (palms facing each other) bring the dumbbells to your knees, then carefully lie back and use your knees to help guide the weights into starting position beside your lower chest.
Start with your upper arms roughly 45 degrees from your torso and elbows slightly below bench level; wrists neutral and dumbbells aligned over the lower chest.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells under control until your upper arms are just below parallel to the bench or the dumbbells are close to the lower chest/rib area.
Exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together, maintaining the neutral grip, until your arms are nearly extended—do not lock the elbows aggressively.
Keep your core engaged and glutes on the bench to avoid arching the lower back; maintain a steady, controlled tempo on both eccentric and concentric phases.
Avoid flaring the elbows out perpendicular to the body; keep them slightly tucked to reduce shoulder stress and emphasize the pectorals.
Use an appropriate weight you can control for the desired reps; if using heavy loads, have a spotter help lift the dumbbells into and out of the start position.
exercises.description: A decline dumbbell hammer press is performed lying supine on a decline bench while pressing dumbbells with a neutral (palms-facing) grip; the decline angle emphasizes the lower portion of the pectorals while the neutral grip reduces shoulder strain. Controlled lowering and pressing works the chest through a full range while triceps and anterior delts assist and forearms maintain grip.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and lower back in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and extend the arms straight above your chest, palms facing each other.
Keep a slight, fixed bend in the elbows throughout the movement (do not lock the elbows).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, maintaining the slight elbow bend until you feel a stretch in the chest or your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor.
Keep the shoulder blades stable on the bench (no excessive shrugging) and avoid letting the dumbbells travel behind the plane of your torso.
Pause briefly at the bottom under control, then exhale and bring the dumbbells back up along the same arc, focusing on squeezing the pectorals until the weights meet above the sternum.
Perform each rep with controlled tempo and avoid bouncing or using momentum; select a weight that allows full control and a safe range of motion.
exercises.description: A supine resistance exercise performed with dumbbells that isolates the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from above the sternum to the sides, producing a deep stretch and concentric squeeze of the pectorals.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Fly
exercises.notes: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30-45 degrees and sit with a dumbbell in each hand resting on your thighs.
Lie back (supine on an incline bench) and plant your feet flat on the floor for stability; bring the dumbbells up to shoulder height with palms facing each other and a slight bend in the elbows.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades slightly (pinch the shoulder blades together) to stabilize the chest and protect the shoulders.
Begin the repetition by slowly lowering the dumbbells out to your sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight elbow bend throughout; lower until you feel a comfortable stretch in the upper chest.
Keep the movement controlled and avoid dropping the weights below the plane of the chest or allowing the elbows to fully lock or flare excessively.
Drive the dumbbells back up by horizontally adducting the shoulders (bringing the arms together) until they meet or nearly meet above the sternum; exhale as you lift.
Pause briefly at the top with tension in the pectorals, then inhale and repeat for the desired reps.
Maintain a neutral neck and avoid shrugging the shoulders; use a weight that allows strict form and full control through both eccentric and concentric phases.
exercises.description: Performed lying back on an inclined bench, the dumbbell incline fly isolates the upper portion of the chest by moving the arms in a wide arc from open to together while maintaining a slight elbow bend. It emphasizes pectoral horizontal adduction with shoulder stabilization from the delts and grip support from the forearms.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

workout.description: Chest/Back workout focusing on bodybuild and endurance training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Wide grip Row
exercises.notes: Attach a wide, straight or slightly curved bar to the low pulley and grasp it with a pronated (palms down) wide grip, arms extended and torso upright.
instructions: Sit on the bench/seat facing the cable machine with feet braced flat on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Attach a wide, straight or slightly curved bar to the low pulley and grasp it with a pronated (palms down) wide grip, arms extended and torso upright.
Set your chest up and maintain a neutral spine with a slight forward lean from the hips — avoid excessive torso rocking or leaning back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows straight back and out to the sides, squeezing your shoulder blades together and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause for 1 second at the peak contraction with elbows pulled past the torso and the upper back engaged.
Slowly extend the arms to return the handle to the start under control until you feel a gentle stretch in the upper back; do not let the weight slam forward.
Breathe out during the pull (concentric) and inhale as you return (eccentric).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders, using excessive torso momentum, or rounding the lower back; reduce weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: A seated cable row using a wide grip attachment that emphasizes the upper back by driving the elbows outward to retract the shoulder blades. Performed seated with feet braced, it develops thicker upper-back musculature and posterior shoulder strength.

exercises.name: Cable Half Kneeling Single Arm Row
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a low to mid pulley and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a low to mid pulley and select an appropriate weight.

Assume a half‑kneeling position beside a bench: the foot of the working side is planted flat on the floor in front, the opposite knee rests on the floor (or pad), and the non‑working hand rests lightly on the bench for support.
Reach forward with the working arm and grasp the handle with a neutral grip, arm extended and shoulder relaxed; maintain an upright torso and neutral spine with core engaged.
Initiate the movement by retracting the scapula and driving the elbow back and down, pulling the handle toward your hip/ribcage — lead with the elbow rather than the hand.
Squeeze the lats and upper back at the end of the concentric phase for 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso stable and avoiding excessive rotation.
Slowly extend the arm back to the start position under control to fully lengthen the lats; maintain core tension throughout.
Breathe out during the pull and inhale as you return; perform the desired reps then switch sides to repeat on the opposite leg/arm.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable row performed from a half‑kneeling position that emphasizes unilateral lat activation while limiting torso rotation. The working arm pulls the handle back toward the ribcage while the non‑working arm/braced knee provide stability.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Reverse Grip Row
exercises.notes: Setup: Attach a straight or easy-curl bar to a low pulley.
instructions: Setup: Attach a straight or easy-curl bar to a low pulley. Stand facing the machine with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart.

Grip and position: Reach forward and grasp the bar with an underhand (supinated) grip about shoulder-width. Hinge at the hips until your torso is angled forward (~30–60° from vertical), keep a slight bend in the knees, and maintain a neutral spine and neck.
Brace your core and pull your shoulder blades down and back to set your scapulae before initiating the row.

Execution (pull): Exhale and pull the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows backward and keeping them close to your body. Focus on initiating the movement with the lats rather than the arms.
Squeeze: At the top of the movement, pause briefly and squeeze the shoulder blades together and the lats.
Return (eccentric): Inhale and slowly extend the arms under control until they are nearly straight and the weight is felt in the lats, avoiding a sudden drop.
Repetitions and tempo: Use a controlled tempo (e.g., 1–2 sec concentric, 2–3 sec eccentric) and avoid using torso momentum or excessive torso rotation.
Cues and safety: Keep the spine neutral, neck aligned, wrists straight, and avoid rounding the lower back. Reduce load if you must jerk the torso to complete a rep.
exercises.description: Standing bent-over row using a low cable and reverse (underhand) grip to pull the handle toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; emphasizes the lats while increasing biceps involvement due to the supinated grip. Maintain a hip hinge and neutral spine to isolate the back muscles and minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying Hammer Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a slight natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades against the bench.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a slight natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades against the bench.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your thighs, palms facing each other (neutral/hammer grip); use your legs to help rack the dumbbells up so they are at chest level with elbows bent.
Set your elbows slightly tucked (about 30–45 degrees from the torso) and wrists neutral so the dumbbell handles remain vertical.
Inhale, brace your core, then press both dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until your arms are nearly extended — do not fully lock the elbows — keeping palms facing each other throughout.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the pectorals, then exhale as you slowly lower the dumbbells back down to chest level with control.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping the movement smooth, shoulders pinned to the bench, and wrists neutral to avoid excessive strain.
To finish, bring the dumbbells back to your thighs and use your legs to safely return them to the rack or floor.
exercises.description: A flat-bench dumbbell press performed with a neutral (hammer) grip to emphasize the chest while reducing shoulder rotation. Uses a palms-facing-each-other position to target the pectorals with added triceps and anterior delt involvement.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Cable Reverse Grip Pulldown
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight, then reach up and grasp the bar with a shoulder-width underhand (supinated) grip, palms facing you.
instructions: Sit on the lat pulldown bench with your feet flat on the floor and knees secured under the thigh pads.
Select an appropriate weight, then reach up and grasp the bar with a shoulder-width underhand (supinated) grip, palms facing you.
Sit upright with a slight lean back (about 10–20 degrees), chest up, and scapulae slightly retracted; maintain a neutral spine throughout.
Inhale, brace your core, then exhale and pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back until the bar reaches your upper chest or collarbone level.
Squeeze your lats and upper back at the bottom of the motion while keeping wrists neutral and avoiding excessive neck or shoulder shrugging.
Slowly control the weight back to the start by allowing the arms to extend fully overhead while maintaining scapular tension (do not fully relax shoulders between reps).
Repeat for the desired reps. Breathe out on the pull and inhale on the return, and avoid using momentum or excessive torso lean.
exercises.description: A seated cable pulldown performed with a supinated (reverse/underhand) grip that emphasizes the lats while increasing biceps involvement. Pull the bar from an overhead position down to the upper chest while keeping the torso stable and shoulders retracted.

exercises.name: Cable Close Grip Front Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Attach a close-grip/V-bar or place hands close together on a narrow bar; grasp the handle with a firm, neutral/pronated grip (hands close together).
instructions: Sit on the lat pulldown bench and secure your thighs under the pads so your torso cannot rise during the movement (seated position).
Attach a close-grip/V-bar or place hands close together on a narrow bar; grasp the handle with a firm, neutral/pronated grip (hands close together).
Start with arms extended overhead, chest up and a slight lean backward from the hips (about 10–20°) to put tension on the lats (slight recline).
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows down and back, leading with the elbows rather than the hands, and pull the bar to the upper chest/upper sternum.
At the bottom of the movement, squeeze your lats and upper back for a brief 1–2 second pause while keeping the torso stable and not using momentum.
Slowly extend your arms back to the start in a controlled manner until your lats are under tension again (full but not locked extension).
Repeat for the desired number of reps; keep core engaged and avoid excessive swinging or pulling with the lower back.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a close grip to emphasize the latissimus dorsi while minimizing shoulder abduction; a narrow grip increases biceps and upper-back involvement. Performed seated with thigh pads to stabilize the body while pulling the handle to the upper chest.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

workout.title: Chest/Back - BODYBUILD/ENDURANCE
workout.description: Chest/Back workout focusing on bodybuild and endurance training.

exercises.name: Barbell Decline Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
instructions: Set a decline bench into a rack and place a barbell at a height you can safely unrack while lying down; secure your feet under the decline foot pads.
Lie supine on the decline bench so your head is lower than your hips and your shoulders and back are flat against the bench; keep feet locked under the pads for stability.
Grasp the bar with a double overhand grip slightly wider than shoulder width (pronated grip); wrists stacked over elbows and shoulders pulled down and back.
Unrack the bar by straightening your arms and moving the bar out over your lower chest; maintain a controlled, tight torso and scapular retraction.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the lower chest/upper abdomen (sternal/xyphoid area) with elbows at roughly a 30–45° angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest, then exhale and press the bar upward along the same path until arms are nearly extended.
Avoid flaring the elbows excessively and keep the feet anchored under the pads; use a spotter for heavy sets and re-rack the bar carefully after the set.
exercises.description: A decline barbell bench press performed lying supine on a decline bench with the head lower than the hips to emphasize the lower portion of the pectorals. The lifter lowers the bar to the lower chest/upper abdomen and presses it back up to full extension.

exercises.name: Cable High Row with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.
instructions: Set two cable pulleys to a high position and attach single-hand or D handles; choose a moderate weight.

Position an incline bench or chest pad in front of the machine so you can sit facing the pulleys with your chest pressed firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust pad height so torso is supported and arms can reach the handles overhead.
Sit tall with chest against the pad, neutral neck, core braced and shoulders down away from ears; reach up and grasp the handles with a neutral or overhand grip.
Begin with arms nearly extended overhead, scapulae slightly protracted; inhale to prepare.
Exhale and pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest by driving your elbows down and out, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the end range.
Hold the contracted position briefly (1–2 seconds) while maintaining a supported chest and no torso rotation.
Slowly release the handles under control, allowing the arms to extend back to the start while keeping tension on the cables and scapulae engaged.
Repeat for desired reps; avoid using momentum, leaning away from the pad, or shrugging the shoulders upward.
exercises.description: Seated chest-supported cable high row performed from dual high pulleys; you pull the handles down and back toward the upper chest while the chest rests against the pad to isolate the upper back. Emphasizes scapular retraction and posterior shoulder activation with reduced lower-back involvement.

exercises.name: Chest Dip (on dip pull-up cage)
exercises.notes: Start by standing between the parallel dip bars and grasp each handle firmly with a neutral grip; step or jump up so your arms are straight and you are supporting your bodyweight (feet can be bent behind you or crossed at the ankles).
instructions: Start by standing between the parallel dip bars and grasp each handle firmly with a neutral grip; step or jump up so your arms are straight and you are supporting your bodyweight (feet can be bent behind you or crossed at the ankles).

Lean your torso forward about 30–45 degrees (hinge at the hips) and lift your chest so your shoulders are slightly in front of your hands to place emphasis on the pectorals; keep your scapulae stable and shoulders down away from your ears.
Brace your core to prevent excessive swinging or arching of the lower back; maintain a controlled, steady body position throughout the set.

Slowly lower your body by bending the elbows, allowing them to move slightly outward (not excessively flared), until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or you reach about 90 degrees at the elbow.

Pause briefly at the bottom while keeping tension in the chest and triceps, then press yourself back up by extending the elbows and driving the chest forward to return to the start position; avoid forcefully locking the elbows.
Breathe in while lowering and exhale while pressing up; perform repetitions with a controlled tempo to maintain tension on the pectorals and reduce momentum.
Finish the set by stepping or lowering your feet to the ground in a controlled manner; if you experience shoulder pain, reduce range of motion or revert to a less forward-leaning dip variation.
exercises.description: A bodyweight dip performed on a dip/pull-up cage with the torso leaned forward to emphasize the chest; the movement lowers the body by bending the elbows and then presses it back up to extend the arms. It primarily targets the pectorals while heavily engaging the triceps and front delts for movement and the upper back for scapular stability.

exercises.name: Cable Single Arm High Row with Chest Support
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a high pulley (above head height) and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a high pulley (above head height) and select an appropriate weight.
Sit on a bench facing the machine with your chest pressed firmly against the vertical chest support pad and feet flat on the floor for stability.
Reach up and grab the handle with a neutral or overhand grip; allow the working arm to start extended overhead while keeping your torso braced against the pad.
Engage your scapula (pull the shoulder blade slightly down and back) before initiating the pull to protect the shoulder.

Pull the handle downward and back in a controlled arc so the elbow travels behind your torso and the hand finishes near the upper chest/side, squeezing the lats and upper back at the end of the concentric phase.
Pause briefly at the contracted position while maintaining chest contact with the pad and avoiding torso rotation or leaning.
Slowly return the handle to the start position with control, allowing the arm to extend fully while maintaining scapular control.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides. Keep breathing: exhale while pulling, inhale while returning.
exercises.description: Seated single-arm high cable row performed with the chest braced against a vertical pad; pull the handle down and back to drive the elbow behind the torso and load the lats and upper back. This isolates one side for focused unilateral strength and scapular retraction.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Wide grip Row
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the bench/seat with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the wide rowing bar with a wide, overhand grip.
instructions: Sit upright on the bench/seat with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the wide rowing bar with a wide, overhand grip.
Start with arms extended, torso upright and chest up, maintaining a neutral spine and slight forward lean from the hips.
Initiate the movement by pulling the shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction), then drive the elbows out and back to bring the bar toward the upper abdomen/lower chest.
Squeeze the upper back and hold the contracted position for 1 second, keeping the neck neutral and avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum.
Slowly return the bar to the start by extending the arms under control while maintaining an engaged core and neutral spine; inhale on the return, exhale during the pull.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movements smooth and avoiding jerking or using the lower back to complete the pull.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip rows train the upper back by pulling a wide handle toward the torso, emphasizing the mid-back and rear shoulder muscles while minimizing lower-back involvement. Use controlled scapular retraction and elbow drive to maximize upper-back engagement.

exercises.name: Lever Bent over Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is inclined forward (approximately 30–45°), keeping the spine neutral and chest slightly up.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and position yourself in front of the plate-loaded lever so the bar/handle is within reach.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is inclined forward (approximately 30–45°), keeping the spine neutral and chest slightly up.
Grasp the bar with a shoulder-width (or slightly narrower) pronated grip and brace your core to prevent rounding of the lower back.
Initiate the rep by pulling your elbows straight back, driving the bar toward your lower ribcage/hip area while retracting and squeezing the shoulder blades.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, keeping the torso stable and avoiding excessive upper-trap shrugging.
Slowly lower the bar under control until your arms are nearly extended and tension is felt in the lats, then prepare for the next rep.
Keep breaths controlled (exhale on the pull, inhale on the return) and maintain a neutral spine throughout; reduce weight if you cannot keep proper hip-hinge form.
exercises.description: A plate-loaded lever version of the bent-over row where you hinge at the hips and pull the bar/handle toward your torso, emphasizing horizontal pulling to develop the lats and mid/upper back. Maintain a neutral spine and stable core while driving elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Wide Hand Push-up
exercises.notes: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.
instructions: Start prone on the floor and place your hands wider than shoulder-width (about 1.5x shoulder width) with fingers splayed for stability; extend your legs and balance on your toes so your body forms a straight line from head to heels (high-plank position).
Brace your core and glutes to keep hips level and maintain a neutral neck (look slightly forward or down).
Inhale, then begin lowering by bending the elbows outward (elbows will flare more than in a close-grip push-up) while keeping the shoulders down and away from the ears.
Lower until your chest is close to the floor or your elbows reach about 90 degrees, keeping the body rigid and avoiding hip sag or excessive lumbar arch.
Pause briefly at the bottom with tension in the pectorals and serratus anterior, then exhale and press through the palms to straighten the arms and return to the starting high-plank position.
Keep controlled tempo, maintain full-body tension, and repeat for the desired repetitions. To reduce difficulty, perform the same movement from the knees with hands placed wide.
exercises.description: A prone bodyweight push-up variation with the hands placed wider than shoulder width to increase chest (pectorals) activation. From a high-plank position you lower the torso toward the floor and push back up while maintaining a straight body line.

exercises.name: Cable Bent Over Single Arm Crossover
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a cable pulley at about shoulder to chest height and select an appropriate weight.

Stand to the side of the pulley with a staggered or shoulder-width stance and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly 30–45° from vertical (slight bend in knees); maintain a neutral spine and brace your abs.
Grab the handle with the hand farthest from the machine, arm extended out to the side and palm facing down or slightly inward; allow a soft bend in the elbow.

Keep your shoulder down and stable, then exhale and pull the handle in a horizontal arc across your body toward the center of your chest, leading with the elbow and squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly with the chest contracted, keeping the torso still (avoid rotating the ribs or shoulders), then inhale and slowly reverse the motion to return the hand to the start under control.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat. Maintain wrist neutrality and controlled tempo throughout.
exercises.description: A single-arm cable movement performed from a bent-over stance that sweeps the working arm across the body to load and compress the chest fibers, emphasizing the inner and upper pectoral region while the torso is stabilized.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

workout.title: Chest/Back - BODYBUILD/ENDURANCE
workout.description: Chest/Back workout focusing on bodybuild and endurance training.

exercises.name: Cable Rear Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the thigh pads so your legs are secured and select an appropriate weight on the stack.
instructions: Adjust the thigh pads so your legs are secured and select an appropriate weight on the stack.
Sit upright on the bench with feet flat, chest up and head in a neutral position; reach overhead and grasp a wide curved lat bar with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Lean back very slightly at the hips (small forward lean is acceptable for comfort), keep the chest lifted and scapulae retracted before initiating the pull.
Exhale and pull the bar down behind your head toward the upper trapezius/occipital area by driving the elbows down and back, focusing on squeezing the lats and shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the bottom with control, avoiding excessive neck extension or forward head movement.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the fully extended start position with controlled tension in the lats; do not let the weight stack slam.
Repeat for the desired reps. Use a front pulldown variation instead if you have shoulder pain or limited mobility to reduce risk.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown where the bar is pulled down behind the head to target the large back muscles. Emphasizes the lats and upper posterior shoulder/upper back while the arms act as secondary drivers.

exercises.name: Lever Outdoor Seated Chest Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the machine seat with your back firmly against the back pad and feet flat on the ground.
instructions: Sit upright on the machine seat with your back firmly against the back pad and feet flat on the ground.
Adjust the seat height so the handles are aligned with the mid-chest (nipple line) when you grip them.
Grasp the handles with a neutral or pronated grip, elbows bent at about 80–90° and held slightly below shoulder height.
Brace your core, keep your chest up and shoulders down/back, and exhale as you press the handles forward and slightly together until your arms are nearly extended (do not lock the elbows).
Pause briefly at full extension, maintaining control and a stable torso.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the starting position, letting the chest stretch but keeping tension on the muscles; avoid letting the weight slam or your shoulders roll forward.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and full shoulder-back contact with the pad throughout the set.
exercises.description: Seated lever chest press performed on an outdoor machine where you push the handles away from the chest to work the pectoral muscles. The movement uses the delts and triceps as secondary movers while you maintain a stable, seated position against the pad.

exercises.name: Lever Bent over Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine or plate-loaded bar to the desired weight and stand with feet hip-width under the bar/platform.
instructions: Set the lever machine or plate-loaded bar to the desired weight and stand with feet hip-width under the bar/platform.
Hinge at the hips with a soft bend in the knees until your torso is approximately 30–45 degrees from vertical (bent-over position); keep the spine neutral and neck in line with the torso.
Grasp the bar with an overhand (or chosen) grip about shoulder-width apart and let your arms hang straight down; engage your core to stabilize the trunk.
Initiate the rep by pulling your elbows back and driving the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen, squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a braced torso, then slowly lower the bar back to the start under control until the arms are extended.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding torso rotation, excessive momentum, or rounding of the lower back throughout the set.
exercises.description: A bent-over lever row performed from a hip-hinged position to drive the loaded bar toward the torso, emphasizing the mid/upper back. It develops pulling strength and upper-back thickness while also engaging the arms and rear shoulders.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Reverse Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and a slight natural arch in the lower back.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with a reverse (supinated) grip so your palms face toward your head; retract your shoulder blades and keep your chest lifted.
Start with the dumbbells at chest level, elbows tucked roughly 30–45 degrees from your torso and forearms vertical.
Inhale, then press the dumbbells upward and slightly back in a controlled path until your arms are extended but not aggressively hyperextended at the elbows.
Exhale as you reach the top, maintain wrist alignment so the weight sits over the forearms, and keep the shoulders down and back.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting chest position under control, feeling the stretch in the pectorals while keeping the reverse grip and elbow position.
Repeat for the desired reps. Use a spotter for heavier loads and avoid excessive wrist extension or flaring of the elbows.
exercises.description: A supine dumbbell press performed with a reverse (supinated) grip that emphasizes the upper/inner pectorals and increases biceps and forearm involvement compared with a standard press.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Back Seated Row
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight, reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or double-handed grip, arms extended and spine straight (no rounded back).
instructions: Sit on the bench or seat facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Select an appropriate weight, reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or double-handed grip, arms extended and spine straight (no rounded back).
Engage your core, maintain an upright torso (straight back), and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction).
Drive your elbows straight back toward your torso while keeping them close to your sides until the handle touches your abdomen or lower chest.
Pause and squeeze your lats/upper back at the end range for 1–2 seconds while avoiding excessive torso lean or trunk rotation.
Slowly extend your arms and allow the cable to return to the start position under control, keeping a straight back throughout the eccentric phase.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the pull and breathing in on the return.
exercises.description: A seated cable row performed with an upright, straight back to pull a handle toward the torso, emphasizing scapular retraction and elbow drive to work the mid- and lower-back muscles. Focus is on controlled pulling and resisting the cable on the return.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Hinge at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (torso roughly parallel to the floor or about 30–45° above parallel), knees slightly bent; this is a bent-over position — brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Let the dumbbells hang straight down from your shoulders with your arms fully extended; keep your head aligned with your spine.
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows back and up, pulling the dumbbells toward your lower ribs or hip area while squeezing your shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the top of the movement, maintaining tightness through the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders excessively.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position with controlled motion until your arms are extended.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, keeping the torso fixed (avoid using momentum or excessive torso rotation) and continue to breathe: exhale while pulling, inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A bent-over row performed with dumbbells where you hinge at the hips and pull the weights toward your torso, emphasizing the lats and upper back. The movement also engages the arms and posterior chain to stabilize the hinged position.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying One Arm Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and knees bent to stabilize the lower body.
Hold a dumbbell in one hand at chest level with a firm grip (palm facing forward or neutral); place your free hand on your chest or the bench for balance.
Retract your shoulder blade slightly on the working side and brace your core to prevent your torso from rotating.
Exhale and press the dumbbell straight up until the arm is nearly extended, keeping a soft bend in the elbow (do not fully lock out).
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell under control back to the chest level, keeping the elbow at about a 45-degree angle relative to the torso.
Maintain steady posture and avoid twisting; reset your scapula and core between reps if needed.
Complete the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat with equal control and setup.
exercises.description: A single-arm dumbbell bench press performed lying supine on a flat bench to train the pectorals unilaterally while the triceps and delts assist; the opposite hand rests for stability. Emphasizes controlled pressing and core stabilization to prevent torso rotation.

exercises.name: Cable Pulldown (pro lat bar)
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
instructions: Sit on the pulldown bench with knees secured under the thigh pads and feet flat on the floor (seated, knees braced).
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the pro-lat bar with a wide, pronated (palms facing away) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Begin tall with a neutral spine and a slight lean back from the hips (not from the lower back), chest lifted and eyes forward.
Inhale, then exhale and initiate the movement by pulling the shoulder blades down and together (scapular retraction) to engage the lats.
Pull the bar down in a controlled arc toward the upper chest (sternal area), leading with the elbows and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause briefly at the bottom and squeeze the lats and upper back without shrugging the shoulders.
Inhale and slowly allow the bar to return to the start position with straight arms, fully extending the shoulders while maintaining a stable torso.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum; keep movement controlled and driven by the lats.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a pro-lat bar performed from a tall seated position, pulling the bar down to the upper chest to target the latissimus dorsi and upper back. Focus is on scapular retraction and controlled eccentric return.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

workout.description: Chest/Back workout focusing on bodybuild and hit training.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Palm Rotational Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you).
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand. Slightly bend the knees and hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor (bent-over position).
Keep a neutral spine and neck (eyes toward the floor a few feet in front of you). Let the dumbbells hang at arm's length beneath your shoulders with a pronated or neutral grip.
Brace your core and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction).

Pull the dumbbells upward toward your lower ribs/waist by driving the elbows up and back. As you ascend, rotate your wrists so the palms turn (for example from pronation/neutral toward supination) at the top of the row.
Squeeze the upper back and rear delts at the top for a brief pause, keeping elbows close to the body and avoiding excessive torso rotation or momentum.
Lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting position while rotating the palms back to the initial orientation, maintaining control throughout.
Repeat for the desired reps. Maintain the hip hinge, slight knee bend, and neutral spine for the entire set.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell row variation where the wrists/palms rotate during the pull to increase scapular retraction and arm involvement. Performed in a hip-hinged position, it emphasizes the upper back while also engaging the lats, rear delts and arms.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with eyes under the bar, feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the low back.
Grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, wrists stacked over elbows and thumbs wrapped around the bar.
Retract and depress the shoulder blades (pinch the shoulder blades together) to create a stable chest platform.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over the chest so it is balanced and steady.
Inhale, lower the bar under control to the mid-chest (sternum) keeping elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso.
Pause briefly with the bar touching or just above the chest while maintaining tightness through the core and legs.
Exhale and drive the bar upward by extending the elbows, pressing the bar back to the start position while keeping wrists neutral.
Avoid excessive elbow flare or bouncing the bar off the chest; maintain a controlled tempo and keep feet planted.
After the set, carefully re-rack the bar by guiding it back onto the bench supports before releasing your grip.

instructions: Set an incline bench to about 30–45° and place it so the foot of the bench is stable.
Lie prone (face down) on the bench with your chest supported, feet on the floor or staggered for balance; keep your head in a neutral position.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms extended straight down toward the floor and palms facing in (neutral grip); brace your core.
Before pulling, depress and slightly retract the shoulder blades to remove slack from the shoulders.
Exhale and pull the dumbbells upward by driving your elbows up and back toward your hips/ribs, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top.
Pause briefly at the top to maximize contraction, keeping the chest on the bench and avoiding torso rotation or thrusting.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly and under control until your arms are nearly fully extended, maintaining shoulder stability.
Repeat for the desired reps; choose a weight that allows strict form without using momentum.
exercises.description: A chest‑supported rowing variation performed prone on an incline bench that emphasizes the upper back and mid‑trapezius/rhomboid region while minimizing torso momentum. Dumbbells are pulled toward the ribs to retract the scapulae and load the back muscles.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.

Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.name: Barbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.
instructions: Set up a flat bench inside a rack so the barbell sits directly above your eyes when you are lying down.

Lie supine on the bench with your head, upper back and glutes in contact with the bench, feet flat on the floor and a small natural arch in the lower back; retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform.
Grip the barbell with a pronated (overhand) grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists should be stacked over the elbows and forearms vertical when the bar is over the chest.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar out over the chest while keeping the scapulae stable; lock the elbows briefly before starting the set.
Inhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner to the mid-chest/sternum (around nipple level), keeping the elbows at about a 45-degree angle from the torso to protect the shoulders.
Allow the bar to touch or come very close to the chest without bouncing; maintain tension in the pectorals and keep the feet and glutes braced.
Exhale and press the bar upward explosively while keeping the bar path slightly arced toward the hips until the arms are fully extended but not hyperextended.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders stable, then begin the next rep. Maintain consistent form and breathing throughout the set.
Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack for heavy sets, and avoid flaring the elbows excessively or letting the lower back lift off the bench.
exercises.description: The barbell bench press is a compound pressing movement performed lying supine on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and anterior delts. It involves lowering a loaded barbell to the chest and pressing it back up to full arm extension with controlled technique.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and the barbell over mid-foot; use an overhand (pronated) grip slightly wider than shoulder width.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly 45 degrees to parallel to the floor; keep the spine neutral (no rounding) and engage the abs and glutes.
Hold the bar with arms extended and shoulders protracted, chest up and eyes looking a few feet in front of you to maintain a neutral neck.
Initiate the rep by driving the elbows back and up, pulling the bar toward your lower chest / upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Keep the elbows close to the body (not flaring excessively) and avoid using momentum from the hips; breathe out as you pull.
Lower the bar slowly and under control to the starting position with arms extended while maintaining the hip hinge and neutral spine; breathe in on the descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, controlling tempo and keeping the torso stable — if you cannot maintain a flat back, reduce weight or stop and reset.
exercises.description: Standing hip-hinged barbell row where you pull a loaded barbell toward the lower chest/upper abdomen; primarily targets the lats and upper back while requiring posterior chain and core stabilization. Maintain a flat back and drive the elbows back to retract the shoulder blades.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Wide grip Row
exercises.notes: Attach a wide, straight or slightly curved bar to the low pulley and grasp it with a pronated (palms down) wide grip, arms extended and torso upright.
instructions: Sit on the bench/seat facing the cable machine with feet braced flat on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Attach a wide, straight or slightly curved bar to the low pulley and grasp it with a pronated (palms down) wide grip, arms extended and torso upright.
Set your chest up and maintain a neutral spine with a slight forward lean from the hips — avoid excessive torso rocking or leaning back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows straight back and out to the sides, squeezing your shoulder blades together and keeping the wrists neutral.
Pause for 1 second at the peak contraction with elbows pulled past the torso and the upper back engaged.
Slowly extend the arms to return the handle to the start under control until you feel a gentle stretch in the upper back; do not let the weight slam forward.
Breathe out during the pull (concentric) and inhale as you return (eccentric).
Avoid shrugging the shoulders, using excessive torso momentum, or rounding the lower back; reduce weight if form breaks down.
exercises.description: A seated cable row using a wide grip attachment that emphasizes the upper back by driving the elbows outward to retract the shoulder blades. Performed seated with feet braced, it develops thicker upper-back musculature and posterior shoulder strength.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.name: Lever One Arm Lateral High Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and chest pad so the handle is reachable overhead when seated and your chest is supported against the pad.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and chest pad so the handle is reachable overhead when seated and your chest is supported against the pad.
Select an appropriate weight and sit with feet flat on the floor, chest braced against the pad, and torso upright (no leaning or twisting).
Reach up with one hand and grasp the high lateral handle with a neutral or overhand grip; keep the opposite hand resting or holding a support if available.

Initiate the movement by retracting the shoulder blade and pulling the handle down and slightly back in a controlled arc toward the side of your upper ribcage, keeping the elbow high and out to emphasize the upper back and rear delts.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the peak contraction for 1–2 seconds while maintaining a stable, upright torso (avoid rotating or jerking).
Slowly reverse the motion to return the handle to the start under control, fully extending the arm without collapsing the shoulder.
Perform the desired number of reps for one side, then switch arms and repeat, keeping form consistent on both sides.

exercises.name: Barbell Pullover
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and your head and upper back supported; grip the barbell with an overhand grip about shoulder-width apart.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and your head and upper back supported; grip the barbell with an overhand grip about shoulder-width apart.
Start with the barbell held directly over your chest with your arms nearly extended and a slight bend in the elbows; brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Slowly lower the barbell in a controlled arc backwards and over your head until you feel a stretch through the chest and lats (keep elbows slightly bent and fixed throughout the motion).
Pause briefly at the bottom of the pullover, then pull the bar back in the same arc to bring it over your chest by contracting your lats and pectorals.
From the chest position, press the barbell straight up overhead until arms are nearly extended to complete the press portion, then lower the bar back to the chest in control.
Repeat the pullover-to-press sequence for the desired reps; maintain core tension, avoid overarching the lower back, and use a spotter for heavier loads.
exercises.description: A supine barbell movement that combines a pullover (bar travels in an arc behind the head) with a press back to overhead, emphasizing a stretch and contraction of the chest while also recruiting the lats and triceps.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and the barbell over the midfoot; grip the bar with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width using an overhand or mixed grip.
Hinge at the hips, push the butt back, and bend the knees slightly so the torso is angled forward (near parallel to the floor), keeping a neutral spine and head in line with the spine.
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to set the upper back before the first rep.
Pull the barbell upward toward the lower chest/upper abdomen by driving the elbows back and keeping them close to the body; focus on squeezing the upper back at the top of the movement.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a flat back, then lower the barbell under control until arms are nearly extended while preserving the hip-hinge position.
Breathe out while pulling the bar up and inhale while lowering it; keep the torso stable and avoid rounding the lower back or using excessive torso momentum.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, and reset your hip-hinge position between sets if you lose posture.
exercises.description: A hip-hinged barbell row that trains the upper back by pulling the loaded barbell toward the torso while maintaining a neutral spine. It also recruits the lats, arms and posterior chain for stability and power.

exercises.name: Lever Pronated Grip Seated Row (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Reach forward and grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip so your palms face down and arms are fully extended.
instructions: Sit on the machine seat with knees bent and feet planted on the footrests; brace your chest against the chest pad (if present) and sit upright.
Reach forward and grasp the handles with a pronated (overhand) grip so your palms face down and arms are fully extended.
Set your spine neutral, retract your shoulder blades slightly, and maintain a tall chest—do not round your back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows straight back, pulling the handles toward your lower chest/upper abdomen while squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the peak contraction and feel the lats and mid-back engage; avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum.
Slowly extend your arms back to the start position in a controlled manner until your arms are nearly straight and the lats are stretched.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, keeping consistent tempo, full range of motion, and a firm grip throughout.
exercises.description: Seated pronated-grip lever row performed on a plate-loaded machine to develop the lats and mid-upper back by pulling a lever toward the torso using an overhand grip. Emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive while maintaining an upright, braced seated position.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

exercises.name: Cable Underhand Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set the seat height and thigh pads so your knees are secured under the pad and select an appropriate weight; attach a straight bar to the high pulley.
instructions: Set the seat height and thigh pads so your knees are secured under the pad and select an appropriate weight; attach a straight bar to the high pulley.
Sit upright on the bench with feet flat, torso tall and slightly braced, then reach up and grasp the bar with an underhand (supinated) grip about shoulder-width apart.
Begin with arms fully extended and scapulae slightly protracted (shoulders relaxed up), inhale, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back.
Pull the bar to the upper chest/clavicle area while keeping the torso stable and leaning back only slightly; focus on contracting the lats and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then slowly return the bar under control to the start position with arms nearly straight and a controlled stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding torso momentum or excessive backward lean; keep wrists neutral and core engaged throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable underhand pulldown (reverse-grip pulldown) is a vertical pulling exercise that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while using a supinated grip to increase biceps involvement. Performed seated at a high pulley, the bar is pulled down to the upper chest with elbows driven down and back.

exercises.name: Cable Front Seated Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the cable pulley to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the cable pulley to chest height and select an appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench facing the machine with your chest pressed against the vertical support pad, feet flat on the floor and knees bent at about 90°.
Reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip, arms fully extended and shoulders relaxed.
Keep your torso upright and core braced; maintain contact with the chest pad throughout the movement.
Exhale and pull the handle straight back by driving your elbows toward your ribcage, leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the end range when your hands reach your torso and you feel a full contraction across the upper back.
Inhale and slowly extend your arms forward in a controlled manner until they are nearly straight, resisting the weight on the return.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding trunk rotation, excessive trunk lean, or shrugging the shoulders; keep the motion controlled and driven by the back, not the arms.
exercises.description: A seated cable row variation performed facing the machine with chest supported by a pad, pulling a single handle straight toward the torso to work the mid/upper back. Emphasis is on scapular retraction and elbow drive to target the upper back and lats.

exercises.name: Lever Decline Chest Press
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat/back pad so that when seated your back is fully supported, your head is lower than your hips (declined position), and the handles sit roughly at the lower-to-mid chest level.
instructions: Adjust the seat/back pad so that when seated your back is fully supported, your head is lower than your hips (declined position), and the handles sit roughly at the lower-to-mid chest level.
Load an appropriate amount of weight on the machine, or set the pin to the desired resistance if using a selectorized unit.
Sit down with your back and head pressed firmly against the pad, feet flat on the foot platform, and chest up. Grip the handles with a neutral or slightly pronated grip, wrists in line with forearms.
Start with elbows bent and the handles near your lower chest. Brace your core and keep your shoulders down and back (avoid excessive shrugging).
Exhale and press the handles forward and slightly downward in a controlled arc until your arms are almost fully extended; avoid locking the elbows at the top.

Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the pectorals, then inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position, controlling the descent so the chest stretches but shoulders do not flare forward.
Perform the desired number of repetitions while keeping constant tension on the chest, maintaining back contact with the pad, and avoiding excessive arching or momentum.
When finished, return the handles to the safety/starting position, release the grips, and step away from the machine before unloading weight if necessary.
exercises.description: A machine-based decline chest press that emphasizes the lower portion of the pectoral muscles by pressing the handles forward and slightly downward while seated on a declined pad. The fixed lever path provides stability and isolates the chest while also engaging the front delts and triceps.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
